Unlike prior methods that rely on implicit signed distance fields and post-hoc layout optimization, PixARMesh jointly predicts object layout and geometry within a unified model, producing coherent and artist-ready meshes in a single forward pass. Building on recent advances in mesh generative models, we augment a point-cloud encoder with pixel-aligned image features and global scene context via cross-attention, enabling accurate spatial reasoning from a single image. Scenes are generated autoregressively from a unified token stream containing context, pose, and mesh, yielding compact meshes with high-fidelity geometry. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that PixARMesh achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality while producing lightweight, high-quality meshes ready for downstream applications.

AI.tldr: PixARMesh is an autoregressive method that reconstructs complete 3D indoor scene meshes from a single RGB image, producing artist-ready meshes in one forward pass without post-processing.
AI.motivation: Prior methods for 3D scene reconstruction rely on implicit signed distance fields and separate layout optimization, which can be complex and require post-processing. There's a need for a unified approach that directly produces coherent, high-quality meshes from single images.
AI.method: The method builds on mesh generative models, using a point-cloud encoder augmented with pixel-aligned image features and global scene context via cross-attention. Scenes are generated autoregressively from a unified token stream containing context, pose, and mesh information.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show PixARMesh achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality while producing lightweight, high-quality meshes ready for downstream applications.
AI.conclusion: PixARMesh successfully demonstrates that joint prediction of object layout and geometry in a unified autoregressive model can produce coherent, artist-ready 3D scene meshes directly from single RGB images, outperforming previous approaches.

title: MM-ISTS: Cooperating Irregularly Sampled Time Series Forecasting with Multimodal Vision-Text LLMs
summary: Irregularly sampled time series (ISTS) are widespread in real-world scenarios, exhibiting asynchronous observations on uneven time intervals across variables. Existing ISTS forecasting methods often solely utilize historical observations to predict future ones while falling short in learning contextual semantics and fine-grained temporal patterns. To address these problems, we achieve MM-ISTS, a multimodal framework augmented by vision-text large language models, that bridges temporal, visual, and textual modalities, facilitating ISTS forecasting. MM-ISTS encompasses a novel two-stage encoding mechanism. In particular, a cross-modal vision-text encoding module is proposed to automatically generate informative visual images and textual data, enabling the capture of intricate temporal patterns and comprehensive contextual understanding, in collaboration with multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). In parallel, ISTS encoding extracts complementary yet enriched temporal features from historical ISTS observations, including multi-view embedding fusion and a temporal-variable encoder. Further, we propose an adaptive query-based feature extractor to compress the learned tokens of MLLMs, filtering out small-scale useful knowledge, which in turn reduces computational costs. In addition, a multimodal alignment module with modality-aware gating is designed to alleviate the modality gap across ISTS, images, and text. Extensive experiments on real data offer insight into the effectiveness of the proposed solutions.

AI.tldr: MM-ISTS is a multimodal framework using vision-text LLMs for irregularly sampled time series forecasting, combining temporal, visual, and textual modalities through cross-modal encoding and adaptive feature extraction.
AI.motivation: Existing ISTS forecasting methods rely solely on historical observations, lacking contextual semantics and fine-grained temporal pattern learning. Real-world ISTS data has asynchronous observations on uneven intervals across variables.
AI.method: Two-stage encoding: 1) Cross-modal vision-text encoding generates informative images/text for temporal patterns using MLLMs, 2) ISTS encoding extracts enriched temporal features. Includes adaptive query-based feature extractor to compress MLLM tokens and multimodal alignment with modality-aware gating.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real data demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed solutions, showing improved forecasting performance for irregularly sampled time series.
AI.conclusion: MM-ISTS successfully bridges temporal, visual, and textual modalities for ISTS forecasting, capturing intricate patterns and contextual understanding while reducing computational costs through adaptive feature extraction.

title: MagRobot:An Open Simulator for Magnetically Navigated Robots
summary: Magnetic navigation systems, including magnetic tracking systems and magnetic actuation systems, have shown great potential for occlusion-free localization and remote control of intracorporeal medical devices and robots in minimally invasive medicine, such as capsule endoscopy and cardiovascular intervention. However, the design of magnetically navigated robots remains heavily reliant on experimental prototyping, which is time-consuming and costly. Furthermore, there is a lack of a consistent experimental environment to compare and benchmark the hardware and algorithms across different magnetic navigation systems. To address these challenges, we propose the first universal open-source simulation platform to facilitate research, design and benchmarking of magnetically navigated robots. Our simulator features an intuitive graphical user interface that enables the user to efficiently design, visualize, and analyze magnetic navigation systems for both rigid and soft robots. The proposed simulator is versatile, which can simulate both magnetic actuation and magnetic tracking tasks in diverse medical applications that involve deformable anatomies. The proposed simulator provides an open development environment, where the user can load third-party anatomical models and customize both hardware and algorithms of magnetic navigation systems. The fidelity of the simulator is validated using both phantom and ex vivo experiments of magnetic navigation of a continuum robot and a capsule robot with diverse magnetic actuation setups. Three use cases of the simulator, i.e., bronchoscopy, endovascular intervention, and gastrointestinal endoscopy, are implemented to demonstrate the functionality of the simulator. It is shown that the configuration and algorithms of magnetic navigation systems can be flexibly designed and optimized for better performance using the simulator.

AI.tldr: First universal open-source simulation platform for designing, visualizing, and benchmarking magnetically navigated medical robots across diverse applications.
AI.motivation: Current design of magnetically navigated robots relies heavily on experimental prototyping (time-consuming and costly), and lacks consistent experimental environments for comparing hardware and algorithms across different magnetic navigation systems.
AI.method: Developed an intuitive GUI-based open-source simulation platform that can simulate both magnetic actuation and tracking tasks for rigid and soft robots, supports third-party anatomical models, and allows customization of hardware and algorithms.
AI.result: Simulator validated using phantom and ex vivo experiments with continuum and capsule robots; demonstrated functionality through three use cases (bronchoscopy, endovascular intervention, gastrointestinal endoscopy); enables flexible design and optimization of magnetic navigation systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed universal open-source simulation platform addresses key challenges in magnetic navigation research by providing a versatile tool for efficient design, visualization, analysis, and benchmarking of magnetically navigated medical robots across diverse applications.

title: InnoAds-Composer: Efficient Condition Composition for E-Commerce Poster Generation
summary: E-commerce product poster generation aims to automatically synthesize a single image that effectively conveys product information by presenting a subject, text, and a designed style. Recent diffusion models with fine-grained and efficient controllability have advanced product poster synthesis, yet they typically rely on multi-stage pipelines, and simultaneous control over subject, text, and style remains underexplored. Such naive multi-stage pipelines also show three issues: poor subject fidelity, inaccurate text, and inconsistent style. To address these issues, we propose InnoAds-Composer, a single-stage framework that enables efficient tri-conditional control tokens over subject, glyph, and style. To alleviate the quadratic overhead introduced by naive tri-conditional token concatenation, we perform importance analysis over layers and timesteps and route each condition only to the most responsive positions, thereby shortening the active token sequence. Besides, to improve the accuracy of Chinese text rendering, we design a Text Feature Enhancement Module (TFEM) that integrates features from both glyph images and glyph crops. To support training and evaluation, we also construct a high-quality e-commerce product poster dataset and benchmark, which is the first dataset that jointly contains subject, text, and style conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that InnoAds-Composer significantly outperforms existing product poster methods without obviously increasing inference latency.

AI.tldr: InnoAds-Composer: A single-stage diffusion framework for e-commerce poster generation with efficient tri-conditional control over subject, text, and style, addressing poor fidelity, inaccurate text, and inconsistent style issues in multi-stage pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion models for e-commerce poster generation rely on multi-stage pipelines with three key issues: poor subject fidelity, inaccurate text rendering, and inconsistent style. Simultaneous control over subject, text, and style remains underexplored, and naive multi-stage approaches introduce quadratic overhead from token concatenation.
AI.method: Proposes InnoAds-Composer, a single-stage framework with efficient tri-conditional control tokens. Uses importance analysis over layers and timesteps to route each condition only to responsive positions, shortening active token sequences. Includes Text Feature Enhancement Module (TFEM) for Chinese text rendering by integrating glyph image and crop features. Also constructs a new high-quality e-commerce poster dataset with joint subject, text, and style conditions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show InnoAds-Composer significantly outperforms existing product poster methods without obvious inference latency increase. The framework effectively addresses the three key issues while maintaining efficiency.
AI.conclusion: InnoAds-Composer provides an effective single-stage solution for e-commerce poster generation with simultaneous tri-conditional control, overcoming limitations of multi-stage pipelines while maintaining computational efficiency through intelligent token routing and text feature enhancement.

title: Restoring Linguistic Grounding in VLA Models via Train-Free Attention Recalibration
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to perform manipulation tasks directly from natural language instructions and are increasingly viewed as a foundation for generalist robotic policies. However, their reliability under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) instructions remains underexplored. In this paper, we reveal a critical failure mode in which VLA policies continue executing visually plausible actions even when the language instruction contradicts the scene. We refer to this phenomenon as linguistic blindness, where VLA policies prioritize visual priors over instruction semantics during action generation. To systematically analyze this issue, we introduce ICBench, a diagnostic benchmark constructed from the LIBERO dataset that probes language-action coupling by injecting controlled OOD instruction contradictions while keeping the visual environment unchanged. Evaluations on three representative VLA architectures, including Pi0, Pi0.5 and OpenVLA OFT, show that these models frequently succeed at tasks despite logically impossible instructions, revealing a strong visual bias in action generation. To mitigate this issue, we propose Instruction-Guided Attention Recalibration (IGAR), a train-free inference-time mechanism that rebalances attention distributions to restore the influence of language instructions. IGAR operates without retraining or architectural modification and can be directly applied to existing VLA models. Experiments across 30 LIBERO tasks demonstrate that IGAR substantially reduces erroneous execution under OOD contradictory instructions while preserving baseline task performance. We additionally validate the approach on a real Franka robotic arm, where IGAR effectively prevents manipulation triggered by inconsistent instructions.

AI.tldr: VLA models suffer from "linguistic blindness" - they execute visually plausible actions even when language instructions contradict the scene, prioritizing visual priors over instruction semantics.
AI.motivation: While VLA models are promising for generalist robotic policies, their reliability under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) instructions is underexplored. The paper aims to investigate a critical failure mode where VLA policies ignore contradictory language instructions in favor of visual cues.
AI.method: 1) Introduce ICBench diagnostic benchmark from LIBERO dataset to probe language-action coupling with controlled OOD instruction contradictions; 2) Evaluate three VLA architectures (Pi0, Pi0.5, OpenVLA OFT); 3) Propose Instruction-Guided Attention Recalibration (IGAR) - a train-free inference-time mechanism that rebalances attention distributions to restore language influence.
AI.result: VLA models frequently succeed at tasks despite logically impossible instructions, revealing strong visual bias. IGAR substantially reduces erroneous execution under OOD contradictory instructions while preserving baseline task performance. Validated on real Franka robotic arm where IGAR prevents manipulation triggered by inconsistent instructions.
AI.conclusion: VLA models exhibit linguistic blindness where they prioritize visual priors over instruction semantics. The proposed IGAR method effectively mitigates this issue without retraining or architectural modifications, improving reliability of VLA policies under OOD instructions.

title: Moving Through Clutter: Scaling Data Collection and Benchmarking for 3D Scene-Aware Humanoid Locomotion via Virtual Reality
summary: Recent advances in humanoid locomotion have enabled dynamic behaviors such as dancing, martial arts, and parkour, yet these capabilities are predominantly demonstrated in open, flat, and obstacle-free settings. In contrast, real-world environments such as homes, offices, and public spaces, are densely cluttered, three-dimensional, and geometrically constrained, requiring scene-aware whole-body coordination, precise balance control, and reasoning over spatial constraints imposed by furniture and household objects. However, humanoid locomotion in cluttered 3D environments remains underexplored, and no public dataset systematically couples full-body human locomotion with the scene geometry that shapes it. To address this gap, we present Moving Through Clutter (MTC), an opensource Virtual Reality (VR) based data collection and evaluation framework for scene-aware humanoid locomotion in cluttered environments. Our system procedurally generates scenes with controllable clutter levels and captures embodiment-consistent, whole-body human motion through immersive VR navigation, which is then automatically retargeted to a humanoid robot model. We further introduce benchmarks that quantify environment clutter level and locomotion performance, including stability and collision safety. Using this framework, we compile a dataset of 348 trajectories across 145 diverse 3D cluttered scenes. The dataset provides a foundation for studying geometry-induced adaptation in humanoid locomotion and developing scene-aware planning and control methods.

AI.tldr: MTC is a VR-based framework for collecting scene-aware humanoid locomotion data in cluttered 3D environments, addressing the gap between current humanoid capabilities in open spaces and real-world cluttered settings.
AI.motivation: Current humanoid locomotion research focuses on open, flat environments, but real-world settings (homes, offices) are densely cluttered and geometrically constrained, requiring scene-aware whole-body coordination that remains underexplored.
AI.method: Developed a VR-based data collection framework that procedurally generates cluttered scenes, captures whole-body human motion through immersive VR navigation, and automatically retargets motion to humanoid robot models with benchmarks for clutter level and locomotion performance.
AI.result: Created a dataset of 348 trajectories across 145 diverse 3D cluttered scenes, providing the first systematic coupling of full-body human locomotion with scene geometry that shapes it.
AI.conclusion: MTC addresses the critical gap in scene-aware humanoid locomotion research and provides a foundation for studying geometry-induced adaptation and developing scene-aware planning and control methods for real-world applications.

title: Mitigating Bias in Concept Bottleneck Models for Fair and Interpretable Image Classification
summary: Ensuring fairness in image classification prevents models from perpetuating and amplifying bias. Concept bottleneck models (CBMs) map images to high-level, human-interpretable concepts before making predictions via a sparse, one-layer classifier. This structure enhances interpretability and, in theory, supports fairness by masking sensitive attribute proxies such as facial features. However, CBM concepts have been known to leak information unrelated to concept semantics and early results reveal only marginal reductions in gender bias on datasets like ImSitu. We propose three bias mitigation techniques to improve fairness in CBMs: 1. Decreasing information leakage using a top-k concept filter, 2. Removing biased concepts, and 3. Adversarial debiasing. Our results outperform prior work in terms of fairness-performance tradeoffs, indicating that our debiased CBM provides a significant step towards fair and interpretable image classification.

AI.tldr: Three bias mitigation techniques for concept bottleneck models improve fairness in image classification while maintaining interpretability.
AI.motivation: Concept bottleneck models (CBMs) theoretically support fairness by masking sensitive attributes, but in practice they leak information and show only marginal bias reduction, requiring better debiasing methods.
AI.method: Three techniques: 1) top-k concept filter to reduce information leakage, 2) removal of biased concepts, and 3) adversarial debiasing applied to CBMs.
AI.result: Outperforms prior work in fairness-performance tradeoffs, providing significant improvement towards fair and interpretable image classification.
AI.conclusion: The proposed debiased CBM represents a significant step forward for achieving both fairness and interpretability in image classification systems.

title: Demystifying KAN for Vision Tasks: The RepKAN Approach
summary: Remote sensing image classification is essential for Earth observation, yet standard CNNs and Transformers often function as uninterpretable black-boxes. We propose RepKAN, a novel architecture that integrates the structural efficiency of CNNs with the non-linear representational power of KANs. By utilizing a dual-path design -- Spatial Linear and Spectral Non-linear -- RepKAN enables the autonomous discovery of class-specific spectral fingerprints and physical interaction manifolds. Experimental results on the EuroSAT and NWPU-RESISC45 datasets demonstrate that RepKAN provides explicit physically interpretable reasoning while outperforming state-of-the-art models. These findings indicate that RepKAN holds significant potential to serve as the backbone for future interpretable visual foundation models.

AI.tldr: RepKAN combines CNNs with Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) for interpretable remote sensing image classification, achieving SOTA performance while providing explicit physical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Standard CNNs and Transformers for remote sensing image classification are uninterpretable black-boxes, lacking explicit physical reasoning about spectral features and interactions.
AI.method: RepKAN integrates CNN structural efficiency with KAN non-linear power using a dual-path design: Spatial Linear path and Spectral Non-linear path to discover class-specific spectral fingerprints and physical interaction manifolds.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art models on EuroSAT and NWPU-RESISC45 datasets while providing explicit physically interpretable reasoning about spectral features.
AI.conclusion: RepKAN has significant potential as a backbone for future interpretable visual foundation models, bridging the gap between performance and interpretability in remote sensing.

title: CollabOD: Collaborative Multi-Backbone with Cross-scale Vision for UAV Small Object Detection
summary: Small object detection in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery is challenging, mainly due to scale variation, structural detail degradation, and limited computational resources. In high-altitude scenarios, fine-grained features are further weakened during hierarchical downsampling and cross-scale fusion, resulting in unstable localization and reduced robustness. To address this issue, we propose CollabOD, a lightweight collaborative detection framework that explicitly preserves structural details and aligns heterogeneous feature streams before multi-scale fusion. The framework integrates Structural Detail Preservation, Cross-Path Feature Alignment, and Localization-Aware Lightweight Design strategies. From the perspectives of image processing, channel structure, and lightweight design, it optimizes the architecture of conventional UAV perception models. The proposed design enhances representation stability while maintaining efficient inference. A unified detail-aware detection head further improves regression robustness without introducing additional deployment overhead. The code is available at: https://github.com/Bai-Xuecheng/CollabOD.

AI.tldr: CollabOD is a lightweight collaborative detection framework for small object detection in UAV imagery that preserves structural details and aligns features before multi-scale fusion.
AI.motivation: Small object detection in UAV imagery faces challenges due to scale variation, structural detail degradation, and limited computational resources. In high-altitude scenarios, fine-grained features are weakened during hierarchical downsampling and cross-scale fusion, leading to unstable localization and reduced robustness.
AI.method: Proposes CollabOD framework with three key strategies: 1) Structural Detail Preservation, 2) Cross-Path Feature Alignment, and 3) Localization-Aware Lightweight Design. It integrates these approaches to optimize conventional UAV perception models from image processing, channel structure, and lightweight design perspectives. Includes a unified detail-aware detection head for improved regression robustness.
AI.result: The framework enhances representation stability while maintaining efficient inference. The detail-aware detection head improves regression robustness without adding deployment overhead.
AI.conclusion: CollabOD effectively addresses small object detection challenges in UAV imagery by preserving structural details and aligning heterogeneous features, achieving better localization stability and robustness while remaining lightweight for practical deployment.

title: Probing Visual Concepts in Lightweight Vision-Language Models for Automated Driving
summary: The use of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in automated driving applications is becoming increasingly common, with the aim of leveraging their reasoning and generalisation capabilities to handle long tail scenarios. However, these models often fail on simple visual questions that are highly relevant to automated driving, and the reasons behind these failures remain poorly understood. In this work, we examine the intermediate activations of VLMs and assess the extent to which specific visual concepts are linearly encoded, with the goal of identifying bottlenecks in the flow of visual information. Specifically, we create counterfactual image sets that differ only in a targeted visual concept and then train linear probes to distinguish between them using the activations of four state-of-the-art (SOTA) VLMs. Our results show that concepts such as the presence of an object or agent in a scene are explicitly and linearly encoded, whereas other spatial visual concepts, such as the orientation of an object or agent, are only implicitly encoded by the spatial structure retained by the vision encoder. In parallel, we observe that in certain cases, even when a concept is linearly encoded in the model's activations, the model still fails to answer correctly. This leads us to identify two failure modes. The first is perceptual failure, where the visual information required to answer a question is not linearly encoded in the model's activations. The second is cognitive failure, where the visual information is present but the model fails to align it correctly with language semantics. Finally, we show that increasing the distance of the object in question quickly degrades the linear separability of the corresponding visual concept. Overall, our findings improve our understanding of failure cases in VLMs on simple visual tasks that are highly relevant to automated driving.

AI.tldr: VLMs fail on simple driving-relevant visual questions despite having reasoning capabilities; researchers analyze intermediate activations to identify two failure modes: perceptual (visual info not linearly encoded) and cognitive (info present but misaligned with language).
AI.motivation: VLMs are increasingly used in automated driving for handling long-tail scenarios, but they often fail on simple visual questions relevant to driving. The reasons for these failures remain poorly understood, prompting investigation into how visual information flows through these models.
AI.method: Researchers examine intermediate activations of four SOTA VLMs, create counterfactual image sets that differ only in targeted visual concepts, and train linear probes to distinguish between them. They assess which concepts are linearly encoded versus implicitly encoded through spatial structure.
AI.result: Concepts like object presence are explicitly linearly encoded, while spatial concepts like orientation are only implicitly encoded. Two failure modes identified: perceptual failure (visual info not linearly encoded) and cognitive failure (info present but misaligned with language). Object distance degrades linear separability.
AI.conclusion: The study improves understanding of VLM failures on simple driving-relevant visual tasks by identifying specific bottlenecks in visual information flow and distinguishing between perceptual and cognitive failure modes, with implications for improving VLM reliability in automated driving applications.

title: Beyond Geometry: Artistic Disparity Synthesis for Immersive 2D-to-3D
summary: Current 2D-to-3D conversion methods achieve geometric accuracy but are artistically deficient, failing to replicate the immersive and emotionally resonant experience of professional 3D cinema. This is because geometric reconstruction paradigms mistake deliberate artistic intent, such as strategic zero-plane shifts for pop-out effects and local depth sculpting, for data noise or ambiguity. This paper argues for a new paradigm: Artistic Disparity Synthesis, shifting the goal from physically accurate disparity estimation to artistically coherent disparity synthesis. We propose Art3D, a preliminary framework exploring this paradigm. Art3D uses a dual-path architecture to decouple global depth parameters (macro-intent) from local artistic effects (visual brushstrokes) and learns from professional 3D film data via indirect supervision. We also introduce a preliminary evaluation method to quantify cinematic alignment. Experiments show our approach demonstrates potential in replicating key local out-of-screen effects and aligning with the global depth styles of cinematic 3D content, laying the groundwork for a new class of artistically-driven conversion tools.

AI.tldr: Art3D proposes artistic disparity synthesis for 2D-to-3D conversion, focusing on replicating cinematic artistic intent rather than geometric accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current 2D-to-3D conversion methods achieve geometric accuracy but fail artistically, missing the immersive experience of professional 3D cinema by mistaking artistic intent (like zero-plane shifts for pop-out effects) for data noise.
AI.method: Art3D uses a dual-path architecture to decouple global depth parameters (macro-intent) from local artistic effects (visual brushstrokes), learning from professional 3D film data via indirect supervision.
AI.result: Experiments show Art3D demonstrates potential in replicating key local out-of-screen effects and aligning with global depth styles of cinematic 3D content.
AI.conclusion: The work lays groundwork for a new class of artistically-driven conversion tools through the Artistic Disparity Synthesis paradigm.

title: TempoSyncDiff: Distilled Temporally-Consistent Diffusion for Low-Latency Audio-Driven Talking Head Generation
summary: Diffusion models have recently advanced photorealistic human synthesis, although practical talking-head generation (THG) remains constrained by high inference latency, temporal instability such as flicker and identity drift, and imperfect audio-visual alignment under challenging speech conditions. This paper introduces TempoSyncDiff, a reference-conditioned latent diffusion framework that explores few-step inference for efficient audio-driven talking-head generation. The approach adopts a teacher-student distillation formulation in which a diffusion teacher trained with a standard noise prediction objective guides a lightweight student denoiser capable of operating with significantly fewer inference steps to improve generation stability. The framework incorporates identity anchoring and temporal regularization designed to mitigate identity drift and frame-to-frame flicker during synthesis, while viseme-based audio conditioning provides coarse lip motion control. Experiments on the LRS3 dataset report denoising-stage component-level metrics relative to VAE reconstructions and preliminary latency characterization, including CPU-only and edge computing measurements and feasibility estimates for edge deployment. The results suggest that distilled diffusion models can retain much of the reconstruction behaviour of a stronger teacher while enabling substantially lower latency inference. The study is positioned as an initial step toward practical diffusion-based talking-head generation under constrained computational settings. GitHub: https://mazumdarsoumya.github.io/TempoSyncDiff

AI.tldr: TempoSyncDiff is a distilled latent diffusion framework for efficient audio-driven talking-head generation that reduces inference latency while maintaining quality through teacher-student distillation, identity anchoring, and temporal regularization.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion models for talking-head generation suffer from high inference latency, temporal instability (flicker and identity drift), and imperfect audio-visual alignment under challenging speech conditions, limiting practical deployment.
AI.method: Reference-conditioned latent diffusion framework with teacher-student distillation: a diffusion teacher trained with standard noise prediction guides a lightweight student denoiser for few-step inference. Incorporates identity anchoring and temporal regularization to mitigate identity drift and flicker, plus viseme-based audio conditioning for coarse lip motion control.
AI.result: Experiments on LRS3 dataset show distilled models retain much reconstruction behavior of stronger teacher while enabling substantially lower latency inference. Component-level metrics relative to VAE reconstructions and preliminary latency characterization (CPU-only and edge computing) suggest feasibility for edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: Distilled diffusion models can achieve practical talking-head generation under constrained computational settings, representing an initial step toward efficient diffusion-based synthesis with reduced latency and improved stability.

title: RODEO: RObotic DEcentralized Organization
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, Accepted at IEEE International Conference on Robotics & Automation (2026)
summary: Robots are improving their autonomy with minimal human supervision. However, auditable actions, transparent decision processes, and new human-robot interaction models are still missing requirements to achieve extended robot autonomy. To tackle these challenges, we propose RODEO (RObotic DEcentralized Organization), a blockchain-based framework that integrates trust and accountability mechanisms for robots. This paper formalizes Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) for service robots. First, it provides a ROS-ETH bridge between the DAO and the robots. Second, it offers templates that enable organizations (e.g., companies, universities) to integrate service robots into their operations. Third, it provides proof-verification mechanisms that allow robot actions to be auditable. In our experimental setup, a mobile robot was deployed as a trash collector in a lab scenario. The robot collects trash and uses a smart bin to sort and dispose of it correctly. Then, the robot submits a proof of the successful operation and is compensated in DAO tokens. Finally, the robot re-invests the acquired funds to purchase battery charging services. Data collected in a three day experiment show that the robot doubled its income and reinvested funds to extend its operating time. The proof validation times of approximately one minute ensured verifiable task execution, while the accumulated robot income successfully funded up to 88 hours of future autonomous operation. The results of this research give insights about how robots and organizations can coordinate tasks and payments with auditable execution proofs and on-chain settlement.

AI.tldr: Blockchain-based framework (RODEO) enables robots to operate as DAOs with auditable actions, proof verification, and token-based compensation for autonomous operations.
AI.motivation: Current robot autonomy lacks auditability, transparent decision processes, and proper human-robot interaction models needed for extended autonomy in organizational settings.
AI.method: Proposed RODEO framework with: 1) ROS-ETH bridge connecting DAOs to robots, 2) organizational templates for service robot integration, 3) proof-verification mechanisms for auditable actions, and 4) token-based compensation system.
AI.result: In 3-day trash collection experiment: robot doubled income, reinvested funds for battery charging, achieved ~1 minute proof validation times, and funded up to 88 hours of future autonomous operation.
AI.conclusion: Blockchain-based DAOs enable robots and organizations to coordinate tasks with auditable execution proofs and on-chain settlement, creating sustainable autonomous operation ecosystems.

title: Pano3DComposer: Feed-Forward Compositional 3D Scene Generation from Single Panoramic Image
summary: Current compositional image-to-3D scene generation approaches construct 3D scenes by time-consuming iterative layout optimization or inflexible joint object-layout generation. Moreover, most methods rely on limited field-of-view perspective images, hindering the creation of complete 360-degree environments. To address these limitations, we design Pano3DComposer, an efficient feed-forward framework for panoramic images. To decouple object generation from layout estimation, we propose a plug-and-play Object-World Transformation Predictor. This module converts the 3D objects generated by off-the-shelf image-to-3D models from local to world coordinates. To achieve this, we adapt the VGGT architecture to Alignment-VGGT by using target object crop, multi-view object renderings and camera parameters to predict the transformation. The predictor is trained using pseudo-geometric supervision to address the shape discrepancy between generated and ground-truth objects. For input images from unseen domains, we further introduce a Coarse-to-Fine (C2F) alignment mechanism for Pano3DComposer that iteratively refines geometric consistency with feedback of scene rendering. Our method achieves superior geometric accuracy for image/text-to-3D tasks on synthetic and real-world datasets. It can generate a high-fidelity 3D scene in approximately 20 seconds on an RTX 4090 GPU. Project page: https://qiuzidian.github.io/pano3dcomposer-page/.

AI.tldr: Pano3DComposer is a fast feed-forward framework that generates complete 360-degree 3D scenes from panoramic images by decoupling object generation from layout estimation using a novel Object-World Transformation Predictor.
AI.motivation: Current methods for compositional image-to-3D scene generation are slow (iterative optimization) or inflexible (joint generation), and limited to narrow field-of-view images, preventing creation of complete 360-degree environments.
AI.method: 1) Object-World Transformation Predictor: Adapts VGGT to Alignment-VGGT using object crops, multi-view renderings, and camera parameters to transform 3D objects from local to world coordinates. 2) Coarse-to-Fine alignment: Iteratively refines geometric consistency for unseen domains using scene rendering feedback. 3) Uses pseudo-geometric supervision to handle shape discrepancies between generated and ground-truth objects.
AI.result: Achieves superior geometric accuracy on synthetic and real-world datasets for image/text-to-3D tasks, generating high-fidelity 3D scenes in approximately 20 seconds on RTX 4090 GPU.
AI.conclusion: Pano3DComposer provides an efficient, feed-forward solution for panoramic 3D scene generation that overcomes limitations of existing methods through decoupled object-layout processing and novel transformation prediction.

title: Text-Driven Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation
summary: Talking Face Generation (TFG) strives to create realistic and emotionally expressive digital faces. While previous TFG works have mastered the creation of naturalistic facial movements, they typically express a fixed target emotion in synthetic videos and lack the ability to exhibit continuously changing and natural expressions like humans do when conveying information. To synthesize realistic videos, we propose a novel task called Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation (EC-TFG), which takes a text segment and an emotion description with varying emotions as driving data, aiming to generate a video where the person speaks the text while reflecting the emotional changes within the description. Alongside this, we introduce a customized model, i.e., Temporal-Intensive Emotion Modulated Talking Face Generation (TIE-TFG), which innovatively manages dynamic emotional variations by employing Temporal-Intensive Emotion Fluctuation Modeling, allowing it to provide emotion variation sequences corresponding to the input text to drive continuous facial expression changes in synthesized videos. Extensive evaluations demonstrate our method's exceptional ability to produce smooth emotion transitions and uphold high-quality visuals and motion authenticity across diverse emotional states.

AI.tldr: Proposes EC-TFG task for generating talking faces with continuous emotional changes, and TIE-TFG model that uses temporal-intensive emotion fluctuation modeling to create smooth emotion transitions in synthetic videos.
AI.motivation: Existing talking face generation methods produce fixed target emotions in videos, lacking the ability to show continuously changing natural expressions like humans do when conveying information.
AI.method: Introduces Temporal-Intensive Emotion Modulated Talking Face Generation (TIE-TFG) with Temporal-Intensive Emotion Fluctuation Modeling that generates emotion variation sequences corresponding to input text to drive continuous facial expression changes.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations demonstrate exceptional ability to produce smooth emotion transitions while maintaining high-quality visuals and motion authenticity across diverse emotional states.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EC-TFG task and TIE-TFG model successfully address the limitation of fixed emotional expressions in talking face generation, enabling realistic videos with continuous emotional variations.

title: Lifelong Embodied Navigation Learning
summary: Embodied navigation agents powered by large language models have shown strong performance on individual tasks but struggle to continually acquire new navigation skills, which suffer from catastrophic forgetting. We formalize this challenge as lifelong embodied navigation learning (LENL), where an agent is required to adapt to a sequence of navigation tasks spanning multiple scenes and diverse user instruction styles, while retaining previously learned knowledge. To tackle this problem, we propose Uni-Walker, a lifelong embodied navigation framework that decouples navigation knowledge into task-shared and task-specific components with Decoder Extension LoRA (DE-LoRA). To learn the shared knowledge, we design a knowledge inheritance strategy and an experts co-activation strategy to facilitate shared knowledge transfer and refinement across multiple navigation tasks. To learn the specific knowledge, we propose an expert subspace orthogonality constraint together and a navigation-specific chain-of-thought reasoning mechanism to capture specific knowledge and enhance instruction-style understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of Uni-Walker for building universal navigation agents with lifelong learning.

AI.tldr: Uni-Walker: A lifelong embodied navigation framework using Decoder Extension LoRA to prevent catastrophic forgetting by separating navigation knowledge into task-shared and task-specific components.
AI.motivation: Current embodied navigation agents powered by LLMs perform well on individual tasks but suffer from catastrophic forgetting when learning new navigation skills sequentially, preventing them from becoming universal navigation agents.
AI.method: Proposes Uni-Walker framework with Decoder Extension LoRA (DE-LoRA) to decouple knowledge into task-shared and task-specific components. Uses knowledge inheritance and experts co-activation for shared knowledge, and expert subspace orthogonality constraint with navigation-specific chain-of-thought reasoning for specific knowledge.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate Uni-Walker's superiority in building universal navigation agents with lifelong learning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Uni-Walker effectively addresses lifelong embodied navigation learning by preventing catastrophic forgetting through knowledge decoupling and specialized strategies for shared and specific knowledge transfer.

title: Multimodal Behavior Tree Generation: A Small Vision-Language Model for Robot Task Planning
summary: Large and small language models have been widely used for robotic task planning. At the same time, vision-language models (VLMs) have successfully tackled problems such as image captioning, scene understanding, and visual question answering. In this work, we combine these two approaches by deploying a compact, open-source multimodal model to generate behavior trees for robotic task planning. The main obstacle to achieving this goal is the lack of an existing dataset that links visual observations and instructions to executable behavior trees. We propose a method to construct such a dataset starting from existing robotic episodes (i.e., Open X-Embodiment), in which a large model serves as a teacher in a multi-stage generation pipeline. We use this dataset to fine-tune VLMs ranging from 500M to 4B parameters via parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). The generated behavior trees, compatible with the BehaviorTree.CPP library, are evaluated both offline, using structural and lexical metrics, and online through the execution of household tasks in a state-of-the-art embodied simulator. Our results demonstrate that our fine-tuned 4B-parameter VLM approaches the performance of state-of-the-art closed-source models, achieving an 87\% success rate while requiring only a fraction of the computational resources.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuned vision-language models can generate executable behavior trees for robotic task planning with 87% success rate, approaching closed-source model performance at lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: Combine language models for robotic planning with vision-language models' visual understanding capabilities, addressing the lack of datasets linking visual observations to executable behavior trees.
AI.method: Construct dataset from existing robotic episodes using large model as teacher, then fine-tune VLMs (500M-4B parameters) via parameter-efficient fine-tuning to generate BehaviorTree.CPP-compatible behavior trees.
AI.result: Fine-tuned 4B-parameter VLM achieves 87% success rate on household tasks in embodied simulator, approaching state-of-the-art closed-source model performance with significantly lower computational resources.
AI.conclusion: Compact open-source multimodal models can effectively generate executable behavior trees for robotic task planning, bridging visual perception with structured robotic control.

title: A Hazard-Informed Data Pipeline for Robotics Physical Safety
comment: 4th International Conference on Automation and Mechatronics Engineering (ICAME 2026)
summary: This report presents a structured Robotics Physical Safety Framework based on explicit asset declaration, systematic vulnerability enumeration, and hazard-driven synthetic data generation. The approach bridges classical risk engineering with modern machine learning pipelines, enabling safety envelope learning grounded in a formalized hazard ontology. The key contribution of this framework is the alignment between classical safety engineering, digital twin simulation, synthetic data generation, and machine learning model training.

AI.tldr: A robotics safety framework combining asset declaration, vulnerability analysis, and synthetic data generation to bridge classical risk engineering with ML safety learning.
AI.motivation: To create a systematic approach for robotics safety that connects traditional risk engineering methods with modern machine learning pipelines, addressing the gap between formal safety analysis and data-driven ML systems.
AI.method: Structured framework based on explicit asset declaration, systematic vulnerability enumeration, and hazard-driven synthetic data generation. Uses formalized hazard ontology to ground safety envelope learning and aligns classical safety engineering with digital twin simulation and ML training.
AI.result: The framework enables safety envelope learning grounded in formal hazard analysis and creates alignment between classical safety engineering, digital twin simulation, synthetic data generation, and machine learning model training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully bridges classical risk engineering with modern ML pipelines, providing a structured approach to robotics physical safety through systematic vulnerability analysis and hazard-driven data generation.

title: Sticky-Glance: Robust Intent Recognition for Human Robot Collaboration via Single-Glance
summary: Gaze is a valuable means of communication for impaired people with extremely limited motor capabilities. However, robust gaze-based intent recognition in multi-object environments is challenging due to gaze noise, micro-saccades, viewpoint changes, and dynamic objects. To address this, we propose an object-centric gaze grounding framework that stabilizes intent through a sticky-glance algorithm, jointly modeling geometric distance and direction trends. The inferred intent remains anchored to the object even under short glances with minimal 3 gaze samples, achieving a tracking rate of 0.94 for dynamic targets and selection accuracy of 0.98 for static targets. We further introduce a continuous shared control and multi-modal interaction paradigm, enabling high-readiness control and human-in-loop feedback, thereby reducing task duration for nearly 10 \%. Experiments across dynamic tracking, multi-perspective alignment, a baseline comparison, user studies, and ablation studies demonstrate improved robustness, efficiency, and reduced workload compared to representative baselines.

AI.tldr: Object-centric gaze grounding framework with sticky-glance algorithm for robust intent recognition in multi-object environments, achieving high tracking and selection accuracy with minimal gaze samples.
AI.motivation: Gaze is valuable for impaired people with limited motor capabilities, but robust gaze-based intent recognition is challenging due to gaze noise, micro-saccades, viewpoint changes, and dynamic objects.
AI.method: Propose object-centric gaze grounding framework with sticky-glance algorithm that jointly models geometric distance and direction trends to stabilize intent, plus continuous shared control and multi-modal interaction paradigm.
AI.result: Achieves tracking rate of 0.94 for dynamic targets and selection accuracy of 0.98 for static targets with minimal 3 gaze samples, reduces task duration by nearly 10%, and demonstrates improved robustness, efficiency, and reduced workload.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables robust gaze-based intent recognition for impaired users in complex environments, providing high-readiness control and human-in-loop feedback for practical assistive applications.

title: Place-it-R1: Unlocking Environment-aware Reasoning Potential of MLLM for Video Object Insertion
summary: Modern video editing techniques have achieved high visual fidelity when inserting video objects. However, they focus on optimizing visual fidelity rather than physical causality, leading to edits that are physically inconsistent with their environment. In this work, we present Place-it-R$1$, an end-to-end framework for video object insertion that unlocks the environment-aware reasoning potential of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Our framework leverages the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning of MLLMs to orchestrate video diffusion, following a Think-then-Place paradigm. To bridge cognitive reasoning and generative execution, we introduce three key innovations: First, MLLM performs physical scene understanding and interaction reasoning, generating environment-aware chain-of-thought tokens and inferring valid insertion regions to explicitly guide the diffusion toward physically plausible insertion. Then, we introduce MLLM-guided Spatial Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), where diffusion outputs are fed back to the MLLM for scoring, enabling visual naturalness. During inference, the MLLM iteratively triggers refinement cycles and elicits adaptive adjustments from the diffusion model, forming a closed-loop that progressively enhances editing quality. Furthermore, we provide two user-selectable modes: a plausibility-oriented flexible mode that permits environment modifications (\eg, generating support structures) to enhance physical plausibility, and a fidelity-oriented standard mode that preserves scene integrity for maximum fidelity, offering users explicit control over the plausibility-fidelity trade-off. Extensive experiments demonstrate Place-it-R1 achieves physically-coherent video object insertion compared with state-of-the-art solutions and commercial models.

AI.tldr: Place-it-R1 is an MLLM-guided framework for physically plausible video object insertion using Chain-of-Thought reasoning and closed-loop refinement.
AI.motivation: Current video editing techniques prioritize visual fidelity over physical causality, resulting in physically inconsistent object insertions that don't interact properly with their environment.
AI.method: Uses MLLM Chain-of-Thought reasoning to understand physical scenes and infer valid insertion regions, guides video diffusion with environment-aware tokens, implements MLLM-guided Spatial DPO for visual naturalness, and creates closed-loop refinement cycles with two user modes (plausibility-oriented flexible and fidelity-oriented standard).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Place-it-R1 achieves physically-coherent video object insertion superior to state-of-the-art solutions and commercial models.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges cognitive reasoning and generative execution for environment-aware video editing, offering users explicit control over the plausibility-fidelity trade-off through its dual-mode design.

title: Towards Driver Behavior Understanding: Weakly-Supervised Risk Perception in Driving Scenes
summary: Achieving zero-collision mobility remains a key objective for intelligent vehicle systems, which requires understanding driver risk perception-a complex cognitive process shaped by voluntary response of the driver to external stimuli and the attentiveness of surrounding road users towards the ego-vehicle. To support progress in this area, we introduce RAID (Risk Assessment In Driving scenes)-a large-scale dataset specifically curated for research on driver risk perception and contextual risk assessment. RAID comprises 4,691 annotated video clips, covering diverse traffic scenarios with labels for driver's intended maneuver, road topology, risk situations (e.g., crossing pedestrians), driver responses, and pedestrian attentiveness. Leveraging RAID, we propose a weakly supervised risk object identification framework that models the relationship between driver's intended maneuver and responses to identify potential risk sources. Additionally, we analyze the role of pedestrian attention in estimating risk and demonstrate the value of the proposed dataset. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves 20.6% and 23.1% performance gains over prior state-of-the-art approaches on the RAID and HDDS datasets, respectively.

AI.tldr: RAID dataset for driver risk perception research with 4,691 annotated video clips and a weakly supervised risk object identification framework that outperforms SOTA by 20-23%.
AI.motivation: Zero-collision mobility requires understanding driver risk perception, which is complex and shaped by driver responses to external stimuli and attentiveness of surrounding road users.
AI.method: Introduce RAID dataset with diverse traffic scenario annotations, then propose a weakly supervised risk object identification framework that models relationship between driver's intended maneuver and responses to identify risk sources.
AI.result: Method achieves 20.6% and 23.1% performance gains over prior state-of-the-art approaches on RAID and HDDS datasets respectively.
AI.conclusion: RAID dataset enables research on driver risk perception and contextual risk assessment, with proposed framework demonstrating significant improvements in risk object identification.

title: VLM-RobustBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Robustness of Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on standard, high-quality datasets, but we still do not fully understand how they perform under real-world image distortions. We present VLM-RobustBench, a benchmark spanning 49 augmentation types across noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric perturbations, evaluated under graded severities (low/mid/high) and binary transforms, yielding 133 corrupted settings. We evaluate VLMs from four families (Qwen, InternVL, Molmo, Gemma) on two complementary benchmarks: MMBench (visually grounded) and MMMU-Pro (reasoning-oriented). Our results reveal that visual severity is a weak predictor of difficulty: low-severity spatial perturbations often degrade performance more than visually severe photometric corruptions. In particular, low-severity glass_blur reduces MMBench accuracy by about 8 pp on average across models, while the largest drops arise from resampling and geometric distortions (e.g., upsample, elastic_transform), reaching up to 34 pp. Overall, our findings suggest current VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile, motivating the definition of novel robustness evaluation protocols and training regimes that emphasize resampling and geometric invariances.

AI.tldr: VLM-RobustBench evaluates vision-language models on 49 image distortions across 133 settings, finding VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile, with geometric perturbations causing largest performance drops.
AI.motivation: While VLMs perform well on standard datasets, we lack understanding of their robustness to real-world image distortions. The paper aims to systematically evaluate VLM performance across diverse corruption types and severities.
AI.method: Created VLM-RobustBench with 49 augmentation types across noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric perturbations, evaluated under graded severities and binary transforms (133 settings). Tested VLMs from four families (Qwen, InternVL, Molmo, Gemma) on MMBench (visually grounded) and MMMU-Pro (reasoning-oriented) benchmarks.
AI.result: Visual severity poorly predicts difficulty - low-severity spatial perturbations degrade performance more than severe photometric corruptions. Glass_blur (low severity) reduces MMBench accuracy by ~8pp average. Largest drops from resampling and geometric distortions (upsample, elastic_transform) up to 34pp. Current VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile.
AI.conclusion: Findings motivate new robustness evaluation protocols and training regimes emphasizing resampling and geometric invariances, as current VLMs show significant vulnerability to spatial perturbations despite strong semantic understanding.

title: Dual-Agent Multiple-Model Reinforcement Learning for Event-Triggered Human-Robot Co-Adaptation in Decoupled Task Spaces
summary: This paper presents a shared-control rehabilitation policy for a custom 6-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) upper-limb robot that decomposes complex reaching tasks into decoupled spatial axes. The patient governs the primary reaching direction using binary commands, while the robot autonomously manages orthogonal corrective motions. Because traditional fixed-frequency control often induces trajectory oscillations due to variable inverse-kinematics execution times, an event-driven progression strategy is proposed. This architecture triggers subsequent control actions only when the end-effector enters an admission sphere centred on the immediate target waypoint, and was validated in a semi-virtual setup linking a physical pressure sensor to a MuJoCo simulation. To optimise human--robot co-adaptation safely and efficiently, this study introduces Dual Agent Multiple Model Reinforcement Learning (DAMMRL). This framework discretises decision characteristics: the human agent selects the admission sphere radius to reflect their inherent speed--accuracy trade-off, while the robot agent dynamically adjusts its 3D Cartesian step magnitudes to complement the user's cognitive state. Trained in simulation and deployed across mixed environments, this event-triggered DAMMRL approach effectively suppresses waypoint chatter, balances spatial precision with temporal efficiency, and significantly improves success rates in object acquisition tasks.

AI.tldr: Event-driven shared-control rehabilitation robot uses DAMMRL to optimize human-robot co-adaptation for upper-limb reaching tasks
AI.motivation: Traditional fixed-frequency control in rehabilitation robots causes trajectory oscillations due to variable inverse-kinematics execution times, requiring better human-robot co-adaptation
AI.method: Event-driven progression with admission spheres, plus Dual Agent Multiple Model Reinforcement Learning (DAMMRL) where human selects sphere radius and robot adjusts Cartesian steps
AI.result: Suppresses waypoint chatter, balances spatial precision with temporal efficiency, and significantly improves success rates in object acquisition tasks
AI.conclusion: Event-triggered DAMMRL approach effectively optimizes human-robot co-adaptation for upper-limb rehabilitation tasks

title: Beyond Static Frames: Temporal Aggregate-and-Restore Vision Transformer for Human Pose Estimation
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have recently achieved state-of-the-art performance in 2D human pose estimation due to their strong global modeling capability. However, existing ViT-based pose estimators are designed for static images and process each frame independently, thereby ignoring the temporal coherence that exists in video sequences. This limitation often results in unstable predictions, especially in challenging scenes involving motion blur, occlusion, or defocus. In this paper, we propose TAR-ViTPose, a novel Temporal Aggregate-and-Restore Vision Transformer tailored for video-based 2D human pose estimation. TAR-ViTPose enhances static ViT representations by aggregating temporal cues across frames in a plug-and-play manner, leading to more robust and accurate pose estimation. To effectively aggregate joint-specific features that are temporally aligned across frames, we introduce a joint-centric temporal aggregation (JTA) that assigns each joint a learnable query token to selectively attend to its corresponding regions from neighboring frames. Furthermore, we develop a global restoring attention (GRA) to restore the aggregated temporal features back into the token sequence of the current frame, enriching its pose representation while fully preserving global context for precise keypoint localization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TAR-ViTPose substantially improves upon the single-frame baseline ViTPose, achieving a +2.3 mAP gain on the PoseTrack2017 benchmark. Moreover, our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art video-based methods, while also achieving a noticeably higher runtime frame rate in real-world applications. Project page: https://github.com/zgspose/TARViTPose.

AI.tldr: TAR-ViTPose: A temporal Vision Transformer for video pose estimation that aggregates temporal cues across frames to improve accuracy and stability over single-frame methods.
AI.motivation: Existing ViT-based pose estimators process each frame independently, ignoring temporal coherence in videos, leading to unstable predictions in challenging scenes with motion blur, occlusion, or defocus.
AI.method: Proposes TAR-ViTPose with two key components: 1) Joint-centric temporal aggregation (JTA) using learnable query tokens for each joint to attend to corresponding regions across frames, and 2) Global restoring attention (GRA) to restore aggregated temporal features back into current frame tokens while preserving global context.
AI.result: Achieves +2.3 mAP gain over single-frame ViTPose baseline on PoseTrack2017, outperforms existing state-of-the-art video-based methods, and achieves higher runtime frame rate in real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: TAR-ViTPose effectively leverages temporal information in videos for more robust and accurate 2D human pose estimation while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Reflective Flow Sampling Enhancement
summary: The growing demand for text-to-image generation has led to rapid advances in generative modeling. Recently, text-to-image diffusion models trained with flow matching algorithms, such as FLUX, have achieved remarkable progress and emerged as strong alternatives to conventional diffusion models. At the same time, inference-time enhancement strategies have been shown to improve the generation quality and text-prompt alignment of text-to-image diffusion models. However, these techniques are mainly applicable to conventional diffusion models and usually fail to perform well on flow models. To bridge this gap, we propose Reflective Flow Sampling (RF-Sampling), a theoretically-grounded and training-free inference enhancement framework explicitly designed for flow models, especially for the CFG-distilled variants (i.e., models distilled from CFG guidance techniques), like FLUX. Departing from heuristic interpretations, we provide a formal derivation proving that RF-Sampling implicitly performs gradient ascent on the text-image alignment score. By leveraging a linear combination of textual representations and integrating them with flow inversion, RF-Sampling allows the model to explore noise spaces that are more consistent with the input prompt. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that RF-Sampling consistently improves both generation quality and prompt alignment. Moreover, RF-Sampling is also the first inference enhancement method that can exhibit test-time scaling ability to some extent on FLUX.

AI.tldr: RF-Sampling is a training-free inference enhancement framework for flow matching text-to-image models like FLUX that improves generation quality and prompt alignment through reflective sampling and flow inversion.
AI.motivation: Existing inference-time enhancement strategies work well for conventional diffusion models but fail on flow models like FLUX, creating a gap that needs to be addressed for flow-based text-to-image generation.
AI.method: Reflective Flow Sampling (RF-Sampling) uses a theoretically-grounded approach with formal derivation showing it performs gradient ascent on text-image alignment score. It combines textual representations with flow inversion to explore noise spaces more consistent with input prompts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show RF-Sampling consistently improves generation quality and prompt alignment across multiple benchmarks. It's the first inference enhancement method to exhibit test-time scaling ability on FLUX models.
AI.conclusion: RF-Sampling successfully bridges the gap for inference enhancement in flow models, providing a training-free solution that improves both quality and alignment while offering theoretical grounding and practical benefits for CFG-distilled flow models.

title: DreamToNav: Generalizable Navigation for Robots via Generative Video Planning
summary: We present DreamToNav, a novel autonomous robot framework that uses generative video models to enable intuitive, human-in-the-loop control. Instead of relying on rigid waypoint navigation, users provide natural language prompts (e.g. ``Follow the person carefully''), which the system translates into executable motion. Our pipeline first employs Qwen 2.5-VL-7B-Instruct to refine vague user instructions into precise visual descriptions. These descriptions condition NVIDIA Cosmos 2.5, a state-of-the-art video foundation model, to synthesize a physically consistent video sequence of the robot performing the task. From this synthetic video, we extract a valid kinematic path using visual pose estimation, robot detection and trajectory recovery. By treating video generation as a planning engine, DreamToNav allows robots to visually "dream" complex behaviors before executing them, providing a unified framework for obstacle avoidance and goal-directed navigation without task-specific engineering. We evaluate the approach on both a wheeled mobile robot and a quadruped robot in indoor navigation tasks. DreamToNav achieves a success rate of 76.7%, with final goal errors typically within 0.05-0.10 m and trajectory tracking errors below 0.15 m. These results demonstrate that trajectories extracted from generative video predictions can be reliably executed on physical robots across different locomotion platforms.

AI.tldr: DreamToNav uses generative video models to translate natural language instructions into robot motion by having the robot "dream" the task in synthetic video before execution.
AI.motivation: To enable intuitive human-robot interaction through natural language instructions instead of rigid waypoint navigation, allowing users to provide vague prompts like "Follow the person carefully" that the system can interpret and execute.
AI.method: Pipeline refines vague language prompts into precise visual descriptions using Qwen 2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, then uses NVIDIA Cosmos 2.5 to generate physically consistent videos of the robot performing the task. From synthetic videos, extracts kinematic paths using visual pose estimation, robot detection, and trajectory recovery.
AI.result: Achieves 76.7% success rate on indoor navigation tasks with wheeled mobile robot and quadruped robot, with final goal errors within 0.05-0.10 m and trajectory tracking errors below 0.15 m.
AI.conclusion: Trajectories extracted from generative video predictions can be reliably executed on physical robots across different locomotion platforms, providing a unified framework for obstacle avoidance and goal-directed navigation without task-specific engineering.

title: FTSplat: Feed-forward Triangle Splatting Network
summary: High-fidelity three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction is essential for robotics and simulation. While Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieve impressive rendering quality, their reliance on time-consuming per-scene optimization limits real-time deployment. Emerging feed-forward Gaussian splatting methods improve efficiency but often lack explicit, manifold geometry required for direct simulation. To address these limitations, we propose a feed-forward framework for triangle primitive generation that directly predicts continuous triangle surfaces from calibrated multi-view images. Our method produces simulation-ready models in a single forward pass, obviating the need for per-scene optimization or post-processing. We introduce a pixel-aligned triangle generation module and incorporate relative 3D point cloud supervision to enhance geometric learning stability and consistency. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves efficient reconstruction while maintaining seamless compatibility with standard graphics and robotic simulators.

AI.tldr: A feed-forward framework that directly predicts continuous triangle surfaces from multi-view images for simulation-ready 3D reconstruction without per-scene optimization.
AI.motivation: Current 3D reconstruction methods like NeRF and 3DGS require time-consuming per-scene optimization and lack explicit, manifold geometry needed for direct simulation in robotics and graphics applications.
AI.method: Proposes a feed-forward framework with pixel-aligned triangle generation module and relative 3D point cloud supervision to directly predict continuous triangle surfaces from calibrated multi-view images in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Achieves efficient reconstruction while maintaining seamless compatibility with standard graphics and robotic simulators, producing simulation-ready models without per-scene optimization or post-processing.
AI.conclusion: The method enables real-time deployment of high-fidelity 3D reconstruction for robotics and simulation by providing explicit triangle geometry in a feed-forward manner, overcoming limitations of optimization-based approaches.

title: Contrastive-to-Self-Supervised: A Two-Stage Framework for Script Similarity Learning
summary: Learning similarity metrics for glyphs and writing systems faces a fundamental challenge: while individual graphemes within invented alphabets can be reliably labeled, the historical relationships between different scripts remain uncertain and contested. We propose a two-stage framework that addresses this epistemological constraint. First, we train an encoder with contrastive loss on labeled invented alphabets, establishing a teacher model with robust discriminative features. Second, we extend to historically attested scripts through teacher-student distillation, where the student learns unsupervised representations guided by the teacher's knowledge but free to discover latent cross-script similarities. The asymmetric setup enables the student to learn deformation-invariant embeddings while inheriting discriminative structure from clean examples. Our approach bridges supervised contrastive learning and unsupervised discovery, enabling both hard boundaries between distinct systems and soft similarities reflecting potential historical influences. Experiments on diverse writing systems demonstrate effective few-shot glyph recognition and meaningful script clustering without requiring ground-truth evolutionary relationships.

AI.tldr: Two-stage framework for learning glyph similarities: supervised contrastive learning on invented alphabets, then teacher-student distillation to historical scripts for unsupervised discovery of cross-script relationships.
AI.motivation: Learning similarity metrics for glyphs and writing systems is challenging because while individual graphemes in invented alphabets can be reliably labeled, historical relationships between different scripts remain uncertain and contested.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: 1) Train encoder with contrastive loss on labeled invented alphabets to create teacher model with robust discriminative features. 2) Extend to historically attested scripts through teacher-student distillation, where student learns unsupervised representations guided by teacher's knowledge but free to discover latent cross-script similarities.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse writing systems demonstrate effective few-shot glyph recognition and meaningful script clustering without requiring ground-truth evolutionary relationships.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges supervised contrastive learning and unsupervised discovery, enabling both hard boundaries between distinct systems and soft similarities reflecting potential historical influences.

title: KISS-IMU: Self-supervised Inertial Odometry with Motion-balanced Learning and Uncertainty-aware Inference
summary: Inertial measurement units (IMUs), which provide high-frequency linear acceleration and angular velocity measurements, serve as fundamental sensing modalities in robotic systems. Recent advances in deep neural networks have led to remarkable progress in inertial odometry. However, the heavy reliance on ground truth data during training fundamentally limits scalability and generalization to unseen and diverse environments. We propose KISS-IMU, a novel self-supervised inertial odometry framework that eliminates ground truth dependency by leveraging simple LiDAR-based ICP registration and pose graph optimization as a supervisory signal. Our approach embodies two key principles: keeping the IMU stable through motion-aware balanced training and keeping the IMU strong through uncertainty-driven adaptive weighting during inference. To evaluate performance across diverse motion patterns and scenarios, we conducted comprehensive experiments on various real-world platforms, including quadruped robots. Importantly, we train only the IMU network in a self-supervised manner, with LiDAR serving solely as a lightweight supervisory signal rather than requiring additional learnable processes. This design enables the framework to ensure robustness without relying on joint multi-modal learning or ground truth supervision. The supplementary materials are available at https://sparolab.github.io/research/kiss_imu.

AI.tldr: KISS-IMU is a self-supervised inertial odometry framework that eliminates ground truth dependency by using LiDAR-based ICP registration and pose graph optimization as supervisory signals, enabling robust performance across diverse environments.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning approaches for inertial odometry heavily rely on ground truth data during training, which limits scalability and generalization to unseen and diverse environments. There's a need for methods that can learn robust inertial odometry without ground truth supervision.
AI.method: The framework uses LiDAR-based ICP registration and pose graph optimization as a lightweight supervisory signal for self-supervised training of IMU networks. It employs two key principles: keeping the IMU stable through motion-aware balanced training, and keeping the IMU strong through uncertainty-driven adaptive weighting during inference. The approach trains only the IMU network without requiring additional learnable processes for LiDAR.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on various real-world platforms including quadruped robots demonstrate the framework's effectiveness across diverse motion patterns and scenarios. The method achieves robust performance without relying on joint multi-modal learning or ground truth supervision.
AI.conclusion: KISS-IMU presents a scalable and generalizable self-supervised inertial odometry framework that eliminates ground truth dependency while maintaining robustness across diverse environments, making it suitable for real-world robotic applications.

title: OD-RASE: Ontology-Driven Risk Assessment and Safety Enhancement for Autonomous Driving
summary: Although autonomous driving systems demonstrate high perception performance, they still face limitations when handling rare situations or complex road structures. Such road infrastructures are designed for human drivers, safety improvements are typically introduced only after accidents occur. This reactive approach poses a significant challenge for autonomous systems, which require proactive risk mitigation. To address this issue, we propose OD-RASE, a framework for enhancing the safety of autonomous driving systems by detecting road structures that cause traffic accidents and connecting these findings to infrastructure development. First, we formalize an ontology based on specialized domain knowledge of road traffic systems. In parallel, we generate infrastructure improvement proposals using a large-scale visual language model (LVLM) and use ontology-driven data filtering to enhance their reliability. This process automatically annotates improvement proposals on pre-accident road images, leading to the construction of a new dataset. Furthermore, we introduce the Baseline approach (OD-RASE model), which leverages LVLM and a diffusion model to produce both infrastructure improvement proposals and generated images of the improved road environment. Our experiments demonstrate that ontology-driven data filtering enables highly accurate prediction of accident-causing road structures and the corresponding improvement plans. We believe that this work contributes to the overall safety of traffic environments and marks an important step toward the broader adoption of autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: OD-RASE is a framework that uses ontology and large visual language models to proactively identify accident-prone road structures and generate infrastructure improvement proposals for autonomous driving safety.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous driving systems have limitations in handling rare situations and complex road structures. Road infrastructure improvements are typically reactive (after accidents), but autonomous systems need proactive risk mitigation.
