Introduces two interaction strategies: Iterative Anchoring (users lock verified placements) and Continuous Interactive Refinement (users correct errors and guide the system). The hybrid approach allows iterative refinement toward semantically and geometrically coherent assemblies.
AI.result: Experiments on several RePAIR groups demonstrate the hybrid approach substantially outperforms both fully automatic and manual baselines in accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The human-in-the-loop framework offers a practical solution for large-scale expert-in-the-loop artifact reassembly, effectively addressing the complexity and scale of real-world cultural heritage reconstruction.

title: DiffInf: Influence-Guided Diffusion for Supervision Alignment in Facial Attribute Learning
summary: Facial attribute classification relies on large-scale annotated datasets in which many traits, such as age and expression, are inherently ambiguous and continuous but are discretized into categorical labels. Annotation inconsistencies arise from subjectivity and visual confounders such as pose, illumination, expression, and demographic variation, creating mismatch between images and assigned labels. These inconsistencies introduce supervision errors that impair representation learning and degrade downstream prediction. We introduce DiffInf, a self-influence--guided diffusion framework for mitigating annotation inconsistencies in facial attribute learning. We first train a baseline classifier and compute sample-wise self-influence scores using a practical first-order approximation to identify training instances that disproportionately destabilize optimization. Instead of discarding these influential samples, we apply targeted generative correction via a latent diffusion autoencoder to better align visual content with assigned labels while preserving identity and realism. To enable differentiable guidance during correction, we train a lightweight predictor of high-influence membership and use it as a surrogate influence regularizer. The edited samples replace the originals, yielding an influence-refined dataset of unchanged size. Across multi-class facial attribute classification, DiffInf consistently improves generalization compared with standard noisy-label training, robust optimization baselines, and influence-based filtering. Our results demonstrate that repairing influential annotation inconsistencies at the image level enhances downstream facial attribute classification without sacrificing distributional coverage.

AI.tldr: DiffInf uses self-influence scoring and diffusion-based image editing to fix annotation inconsistencies in facial attribute datasets, improving classification without discarding data.
AI.motivation: Facial attribute datasets have annotation inconsistencies due to subjective labeling and visual confounders (pose, illumination, expression, demographics), which create supervision errors that impair representation learning and degrade downstream prediction performance.
AI.method: 1. Train baseline classifier and compute self-influence scores using first-order approximation to identify influential training instances. 2. Apply targeted generative correction via latent diffusion autoencoder to align visual content with assigned labels while preserving identity/realism. 3. Train lightweight predictor of high-influence membership as surrogate influence regularizer for differentiable guidance. 4. Replace original samples with edited ones to create influence-refined dataset of same size.
AI.result: DiffInf consistently improves generalization across multi-class facial attribute classification compared to standard noisy-label training, robust optimization baselines, and influence-based filtering methods.
AI.conclusion: Repairing influential annotation inconsistencies at the image level via generative correction enhances downstream facial attribute classification without sacrificing distributional coverage, offering a better alternative to discarding problematic samples.

title: Locating and Editing Figure-Ground Organization in Vision Transformers
summary: Vision Transformers must resolve figure-ground organization by choosing between completions driven by local geometric evidence and those favored by global organizational priors, giving rise to a characteristic perceptual ambiguity. We aim to locate where the canonical Gestalt prior convexity is realized within the internal components of BEiT. Using a controlled perceptual conflict based on synthetic shapes of darts, we systematically mask regions that equally admit either a concave completion or a convex completion. We show that BEiT reliably favors convex completion under this competition. Projecting internal activations into the model's discrete visual codebook space via logit attribution reveals that this preference is governed by identifiable functional units within transformer substructures. Specifically, we find that figure-ground organization is ambiguous through early and intermediate layers and resolves abruptly in later layers. By decomposing the direct effect of attention heads, we identify head L0H9 acting as an early seed, introducing a weak bias toward convexity. Downscaling this single attention head shifts the distributional mass of the perceptual conflict across a continuous decision boundary, allowing concave evidence to guide completion.

AI.tldr: BEiT vision transformers show a bias toward convex completions in perceptual ambiguity, governed by specific attention heads like L0H9 that act as early seeds for convexity preference.
AI.motivation: To understand where Gestalt perceptual priors (specifically convexity) are realized within vision transformer architectures, using BEiT as a case study for figure-ground organization in ambiguous visual stimuli.
AI.method: Used synthetic dart shapes with masked regions that equally admit concave or convex completions, creating controlled perceptual conflict. Projected internal activations into discrete visual codebook space via logit attribution to trace convexity preference through transformer layers.
AI.result: BEiT reliably favors convex completion under competition. Figure-ground organization remains ambiguous through early/intermediate layers but resolves abruptly in later layers. Head L0H9 acts as an early seed introducing weak convexity bias - downscaling this single head shifts perceptual decisions toward concave completions.
AI.conclusion: Gestalt perceptual priors like convexity are implemented by identifiable functional units within transformer substructures, with specific attention heads serving as early decision seeds that can be manipulated to alter perceptual outcomes.

title: Non-invasive Growth Monitoring of Small Freshwater Fish in Home Aquariums via Stereo Vision
summary: Monitoring fish growth behavior provides relevant information about fish health in aquaculture and home aquariums. Yet, monitoring fish sizes poses different challenges, as fish are small and subject to strong refractive distortions in aquarium environments. Image-based measurement offers a practical, non-invasive alternative that allows frequent monitoring without disturbing the fish. In this paper, we propose a non-invasive refraction-aware stereo vision method to estimate fish length in aquariums. Our approach uses a YOLOv11-Pose network to detect fish and predict anatomical keypoints on the fish in each stereo image. A refraction-aware epipolar constraint accounting for the air-glass-water interfaces enables robust matching, and unreliable detections are removed using a learned quality score. A subsequent refraction-aware 3D triangulation recovers 3D keypoints, from which fish length is measured. We validate our approach on a new stereo dataset of endangered Sulawesi ricefish captured under aquarium-like conditions and demonstrate that filtering low-quality detections is essential for accurate length estimation. The proposed system offers a simple and practical solution for non-invasive growth monitoring and can be easily applied in home aquariums.

AI.tldr: Non-invasive stereo vision method using refraction-aware 3D triangulation to measure fish length in aquariums, validated on endangered Sulawesi ricefish.
AI.motivation: Monitoring fish growth is important for health assessment in aquaculture and home aquariums, but traditional methods are invasive or inaccurate due to refractive distortions in aquarium environments.
AI.method: Uses YOLOv11-Pose for fish detection and keypoint prediction in stereo images, refraction-aware epipolar constraint for robust matching, quality score filtering, and refraction-aware 3D triangulation to recover 3D keypoints for length measurement.
AI.result: Validated on new stereo dataset of endangered Sulawesi ricefish, demonstrating that filtering low-quality detections is essential for accurate length estimation.
AI.conclusion: Proposed system offers simple, practical solution for non-invasive growth monitoring that can be easily applied in home aquariums.

title: What if? Emulative Simulation with World Models for Situated Reasoning
summary: Situated reasoning often relies on active exploration, yet in many real-world scenarios such exploration is infeasible due to physical constraints of robots or safety concerns of visually impaired users. Given only a limited observation, can an agent mentally simulate a future trajectory toward a target situation and answer spatial what-if questions? We introduce WanderDream, the first large-scale dataset designed for the emulative simulation of mental exploration, enabling models to reason without active exploration. WanderDream-Gen comprises 15.8K panoramic videos across 1,088 real scenes from HM3D, ScanNet++, and real-world captures, depicting imagined trajectories from current viewpoints to target situations. WanderDream-QA contains 158K question-answer pairs, covering starting states, paths, and end states along each trajectory to comprehensively evaluate exploration-based reasoning. Extensive experiments with world models and MLLMs demonstrate (1) that mental exploration is essential for situated reasoning, (2) that world models achieve compelling performance on WanderDream-Gen, (3) that imagination substantially facilitates reasoning on WanderDream-QA, and (4) that WanderDream data exhibit remarkable transferability to real-world scenarios. The source code and all data will be released.

AI.tldr: WanderDream is a large-scale dataset for mental exploration simulation, enabling AI models to reason about spatial what-if questions without physical exploration through panoramic videos and QA pairs.
AI.motivation: Real-world spatial reasoning often requires active exploration, but physical constraints (robot limitations, safety concerns for visually impaired users) make actual exploration infeasible. The paper aims to enable agents to mentally simulate future trajectories and answer spatial what-if questions from limited observations.
AI.method: Created two components: WanderDream-Gen (15.8K panoramic videos across 1,088 real scenes from HM3D, ScanNet++, and real-world captures showing imagined trajectories) and WanderDream-QA (158K question-answer pairs covering starting states, paths, and end states). Tested with world models and multimodal large language models (MLLMs).
AI.result: Experiments show: (1) mental exploration is essential for situated reasoning, (2) world models achieve compelling performance on WanderDream-Gen, (3) imagination substantially facilitates reasoning on WanderDream-QA, and (4) WanderDream data exhibits remarkable transferability to real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: WanderDream enables effective mental exploration simulation for spatial reasoning without physical constraints, demonstrating that imagination-based approaches can successfully handle spatial what-if questions in constrained environments.

title: CaTok: Taming Mean Flows for One-Dimensional Causal Image Tokenization
comment: Project website is available in https://sharelab-sii.github.io/catok-web
summary: Autoregressive (AR) language models rely on causal tokenization, but extending this paradigm to vision remains non-trivial. Current visual tokenizers either flatten 2D patches into non-causal sequences or enforce heuristic orderings that misalign with the "next-token prediction" pattern. Recent diffusion autoencoders similarly fall short: conditioning the decoder on all tokens lacks causality, while applying nested dropout mechanism introduces imbalance. To address these challenges, we present CaTok, a 1D causal image tokenizer with a MeanFlow decoder. By selecting tokens over time intervals and binding them to the MeanFlow objective, as illustrated in Fig. 1, CaTok learns causal 1D representations that support both fast one-step generation and high-fidelity multi-step sampling, while naturally capturing diverse visual concepts across token intervals. To further stabilize and accelerate training, we propose a straightforward regularization REPA-A, which aligns encoder features with Vision Foundation Models (VFMs). Experiments demonstrate that CaTok achieves state-of-the-art results on ImageNet reconstruction, reaching 0.75 FID, 22.53 PSNR and 0.674 SSIM with fewer training epochs, and the AR model attains performance comparable to leading approaches.

AI.tldr: CaTok introduces a 1D causal image tokenizer with MeanFlow decoder that enables autoregressive vision modeling by learning causal 1D representations supporting both fast one-step and high-fidelity multi-step image generation.
AI.motivation: Current visual tokenizers either flatten 2D patches into non-causal sequences or use heuristic orderings that misalign with next-token prediction patterns. Diffusion autoencoders also fail to provide proper causality - either conditioning on all tokens lacks causality, or nested dropout creates imbalance.
AI.method: CaTok uses a 1D causal image tokenizer with MeanFlow decoder that selects tokens over time intervals and binds them to the MeanFlow objective. Also introduces REPA-A regularization to align encoder features with Vision Foundation Models for training stability and acceleration.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on ImageNet reconstruction: 0.75 FID, 22.53 PSNR, 0.674 SSIM with fewer training epochs. The AR model attains performance comparable to leading approaches.
AI.conclusion: CaTok successfully bridges the gap between autoregressive language modeling and vision by providing a proper causal tokenization framework that supports efficient image generation while capturing diverse visual concepts across token intervals.

title: Pinterest Canvas: Large-Scale Image Generation at Pinterest
summary: While recent image generation models demonstrate a remarkable ability to handle a wide variety of image generation tasks, this flexibility makes them hard to control via prompting or simple inference adaptation alone, rendering them unsuitable for use cases with strict product requirements. In this paper, we introduce Pinterest Canvas, our large-scale image generation system built to support image editing and enhancement use cases at Pinterest. Canvas is first trained on a diverse, multimodal dataset to produce a foundational diffusion model with broad image-editing capabilities. However, rather than relying on one generic model to handle every downstream task, we instead rapidly fine-tune variants of this base model on task-specific datasets, producing specialized models for individual use cases. We describe key components of Canvas and summarize our best practices for dataset curation, training, and inference. We also showcase task-specific variants through case studies on background enhancement and aspect-ratio outpainting, highlighting how we tackle their specific product requirements. Online A/B experiments demonstrate that our enhanced images receive a significant 18.0% and 12.5% engagement lift, respectively, and comparisons with human raters further validate that our models outperform third-party models on these tasks. Finally, we showcase other Canvas variants, including multi-image scene synthesis and image-to-video generation, demonstrating that our approach can generalize to a wide variety of potential downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Pinterest Canvas is a large-scale image generation system that uses a foundational diffusion model fine-tuned for specific editing tasks, achieving significant engagement lifts and outperforming third-party models.
AI.motivation: While recent image generation models are flexible, they're hard to control via prompting alone and unsuitable for product use cases with strict requirements. Pinterest needs a system that can reliably support specific image editing and enhancement tasks.
AI.method: Train a foundational diffusion model on diverse multimodal data, then rapidly fine-tune task-specific variants on specialized datasets for individual use cases. Key components include dataset curation, training, and inference best practices.
AI.result: Online A/B experiments show 18.0% and 12.5% engagement lifts for background enhancement and aspect-ratio outpainting tasks. Human raters validate that Canvas models outperform third-party models on these specific tasks.
AI.conclusion: The task-specific fine-tuning approach effectively addresses product requirements and generalizes to various downstream tasks including multi-image scene synthesis and image-to-video generation.

title: Training Flow Matching: The Role of Weighting and Parameterization
summary: We study the training objectives of denoising-based generative models, with a particular focus on loss weighting and output parameterization, including noise-, clean image-, and velocity-based formulations. Through a systematic numerical study, we analyze how these training choices interact with the intrinsic dimensionality of the data manifold, model architecture, and dataset size. Our experiments span synthetic datasets with controlled geometry as well as image data, and compare training objectives using quantitative metrics for denoising accuracy (PSNR across noise levels) and generative quality (FID). Rather than proposing a new method, our goal is to disentangle the various factors that matter when training a flow matching model, in order to provide practical insights on design choices.

AI.tldr: Systematic analysis of training objectives for denoising-based generative models, examining loss weighting, output parameterization, and their interactions with data geometry, architecture, and dataset size.
AI.motivation: To disentangle the various factors that matter when training flow matching models, providing practical insights on design choices rather than proposing new methods.
AI.method: Systematic numerical study comparing training objectives (noise-, clean image-, and velocity-based formulations) across synthetic datasets with controlled geometry and image data, using quantitative metrics like PSNR and FID.
AI.result: Analysis of how training choices interact with intrinsic dimensionality of data manifold, model architecture, and dataset size, providing insights on optimal design choices for denoising-based generative models.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical guidance on training objective selection for flow matching models based on data characteristics and available resources, helping practitioners make informed design decisions.

title: GreenRFM: Toward a resource-efficient radiology foundation model
summary: The development of radiology foundation models (RFMs) is hindered by a reliance on brute-force scaling. Existing approaches often directly translate methods for natural images, which prioritize scale over precision and hence lead to brittle and expensive models in clinical practice. To address this, we present a resource-efficient pre-training framework, GreenRFM, that achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our framework ensures robust generalization across diverse patient populations and imaging protocols, reducing computational requirements by orders of magnitude while surpassing complex, parameter-heavy models. These capabilities stem from principled supervision design that aims to maximally utilize supervisory signals via More distilled, Ubiquitous, Semantic-enforcing, and Task-aligning (MUST) supervision, rather than simply piling up the quantity of training data. We offer two GreenRFM configurations: (i) a performant model that establishes a new state-of-the-art using a single 24GB GPU within 24 hours, and (ii) a lightweight model that matches existing benchmarks with 6GB VRAM in 4 hours. We conduct extensive experiments using over 200,000 images from four institutions and of two modalities. GreenRFMs achieve superior performances on chest and abdominal CT datasets, regardless of public or private benchmark, surpassing a range of baseline models. In addition, the results on internal musculoskeletal MRI images show that the same supervision principles transfer between different modalities. Our performance and efficiency challenge the ``scale is all you need'' dogma and democratize the equitable development of state-of-the-art RFMs for clinicians even on a laptop.

AI.tldr: GreenRFM is a resource-efficient radiology foundation model that achieves SOTA performance with dramatically reduced computational requirements through principled supervision design rather than brute-force scaling.
AI.motivation: Current radiology foundation models rely on brute-force scaling, directly translating natural image methods that prioritize scale over precision, leading to brittle and expensive models unsuitable for clinical practice.
AI.method: GreenRFM uses a principled supervision design called MUST (More distilled, Ubiquitous, Semantic-enforcing, and Task-aligning) supervision that maximizes supervisory signals rather than simply scaling training data. Offers two configurations: performant (24GB GPU/24h) and lightweight (6GB VRAM/4h).
AI.result: Achieves superior performance on chest and abdominal CT datasets across public/private benchmarks, surpassing baseline models. Transfers successfully to musculoskeletal MRI. Reduces computational requirements by orders of magnitude while establishing new SOTA.
AI.conclusion: Challenges the "scale is all you need" dogma and democratizes equitable development of SOTA radiology foundation models, making them accessible to clinicians even on laptop hardware.

title: Match4Annotate: Propagating Sparse Video Annotations via Implicit Neural Feature Matching
summary: Acquiring per-frame video annotations remains a primary bottleneck for deploying computer vision in specialized domains such as medical imaging, where expert labeling is slow and costly. Label propagation offers a natural solution, yet existing approaches face fundamental limitations. Video trackers and segmentation models can propagate labels within a single sequence but require per-video initialization and cannot generalize across videos. Classic correspondence pipelines operate on detector-chosen keypoints and struggle in low-texture scenes, while dense feature matching and one-shot segmentation methods enable cross-video propagation but lack spatiotemporal smoothness and unified support for both point and mask annotations. We present Match4Annotate, a lightweight framework for both intra-video and inter-video propagation of point and mask annotations. Our method fits a SIREN-based implicit neural representation to DINOv3 features at test time, producing a continuous, high-resolution spatiotemporal feature field, and learns a smooth implicit deformation field between frame pairs to guide correspondence matching. We evaluate on three challenging clinical ultrasound datasets. Match4Annotate achieves state-of-the-art inter-video propagation, outperforming feature matching and one-shot segmentation baselines, while remaining competitive with specialized trackers for intra-video propagation. Our results show that lightweight, test-time-optimized feature matching pipelines have the potential to offer an efficient and accessible solution for scalable annotation workflows.

AI.tldr: Match4Annotate: A lightweight framework using implicit neural representations and deformation fields for both intra- and inter-video propagation of point and mask annotations in specialized domains like medical imaging.
AI.motivation: Per-frame video annotation is slow and costly in specialized domains like medical imaging. Existing label propagation methods have limitations: video trackers require per-video initialization and lack cross-video generalization, correspondence pipelines struggle in low-texture scenes, and dense feature matching lacks spatiotemporal smoothness.
AI.method: Fits a SIREN-based implicit neural representation to DINOv3 features at test time to create a continuous, high-resolution spatiotemporal feature field. Learns a smooth implicit deformation field between frame pairs to guide correspondence matching for both point and mask annotations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art inter-video propagation on three challenging clinical ultrasound datasets, outperforming feature matching and one-shot segmentation baselines. Remains competitive with specialized trackers for intra-video propagation.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, test-time-optimized feature matching pipelines like Match4Annotate offer an efficient and accessible solution for scalable annotation workflows in specialized domains where expert labeling is costly.

title: Self-Supervised Flow Matching for Scalable Multi-Modal Synthesis
summary: Strong semantic representations improve the convergence and generation quality of diffusion and flow models. Existing approaches largely rely on external models, which require separate training, operate on misaligned objectives, and exhibit unexpected scaling behavior. We argue that this dependence arises from the model's training objective, which poses a denoising task with little incentive to learn semantic representations. We introduce Self-Flow: a self-supervised flow matching paradigm that integrates representation learning within the generative framework. Our key mechanism, Dual-Timestep Scheduling, applies heterogeneous noise levels across tokens, creating an information asymmetry that forces the model to infer missing information from corrupted inputs. This drives learning strong representations alongside generative capabilities without external supervision. Our method generalizes across modalities and enables multi-modal training while following expected scaling laws, achieving superior image, video, and audio generation.

AI.tldr: Self-Flow: self-supervised flow matching that learns semantic representations internally via dual-timestep scheduling, eliminating need for external models and improving multi-modal generation.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion/flow models rely on external models for semantic representations, which require separate training, have misaligned objectives, and show unexpected scaling behavior. This dependence stems from denoising objectives that don't incentivize learning semantic representations.
AI.method: Introduces Self-Flow with Dual-Timestep Scheduling: applies heterogeneous noise levels across tokens to create information asymmetry, forcing the model to infer missing information from corrupted inputs. This integrates representation learning within the generative framework without external supervision.
AI.result: Method generalizes across modalities, enables multi-modal training, follows expected scaling laws, and achieves superior image, video, and audio generation compared to approaches using external models.
AI.conclusion: Self-Flow successfully integrates representation learning with generative modeling through self-supervised flow matching, eliminating dependence on external models while improving performance and scaling behavior across multiple modalities.

title: SCAN: Visual Explanations with Self-Confidence and Analysis Networks
comment: 14 pages, 9 figures, IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) has become essential in computer vision to make the decision-making processes of deep learning models transparent. However, current visual explanation (XAI) methods face a critical trade-off between the high fidelity of architecture-specific methods and the broad applicability of universal ones. This often results in abstract or fragmented explanations and makes it difficult to compare explanatory power across diverse model families, such as CNNs and Transformers. This paper introduces the Self-Confidence and Analysis Networks (SCAN), a novel universal framework that overcomes these limitations for both convolutional neural network and transformer architectures. SCAN utilizes an AutoEncoder-based approach to reconstruct features from a model's intermediate layers. Guided by the Information Bottleneck principle, it generates a high-resolution Self-Confidence Map that identifies information-rich regions. Extensive experiments on diverse architectures and datasets demonstrate that SCAN consistently achieves outstanding performance on various quantitative metrics such as AUC-D, Negative AUC, Drop%, and Win%. Qualitatively, it produces significantly clearer, object-focused explanations than existing methods. By providing a unified framework that is both architecturally universal and highly faithful, SCAN enhances model transparency and offers a more reliable tool for understanding the decision-making processes of complex neural networks.

AI.tldr: SCAN is a universal XAI framework that generates high-resolution self-confidence maps for both CNNs and Transformers, overcoming the trade-off between fidelity and applicability in current methods.
AI.motivation: Current XAI methods face a critical trade-off: architecture-specific methods have high fidelity but limited applicability, while universal methods have broad applicability but often produce abstract/fragmented explanations. This makes it difficult to compare explanations across different model families like CNNs and Transformers.
AI.method: SCAN uses an AutoEncoder-based approach to reconstruct features from intermediate layers, guided by the Information Bottleneck principle. It generates high-resolution Self-Confidence Maps that identify information-rich regions in both CNN and Transformer architectures.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SCAN consistently achieves outstanding performance on quantitative metrics (AUC-D, Negative AUC, Drop%, Win%) and produces significantly clearer, object-focused explanations than existing methods across diverse architectures and datasets.
AI.conclusion: SCAN provides a unified framework that is both architecturally universal and highly faithful, enhancing model transparency and offering a more reliable tool for understanding complex neural network decision-making processes.

title: AV-Unified: A Unified Framework for Audio-visual Scene Understanding
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Multimedia (TMM)
summary: When humans perceive the world, they naturally integrate multiple audio-visual tasks within dynamic, real-world scenes. However, current works such as event localization, parsing, segmentation and question answering are mostly explored individually, making it challenging to comprehensively understand complex audio-visual scenes and explore inter-task relationships. Hence, we propose \textbf{AV-Unified}, a unified framework that enables joint learning across a wide range of audio-visual scene understanding tasks. AV-Unified standardizes the diverse input-output formats of each task and incorporates a multi-scale spatiotemporal perception network to effectively capture audio-visual associations. Specifically, we unify the inputs and outputs of all supported tasks by converting them into sequences of discrete tokens, establishing a shared representation that allows a single architecture to be trained jointly across heterogeneous varied datasets. Considering the varying temporal granularity of audio-visual events, a multi-scale temporal perception module is designed to capture key cues. Meanwhile, to overcome the lack of auditory supervision in the visual domain, we design a cross-modal guidance-based spatial perception module that models spatial audio-visual associations. Furthermore, task-specific text prompts are employed to enhance the model's adaptability and task-awareness. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., AVE, LLP, MUSIC-AVQA, VGG-SS and AVS) demonstrate the effectiveness of AV-Unified across temporal, spatial, and spatiotemporal tasks.

AI.tldr: AV-Unified is a unified framework for joint learning across diverse audio-visual scene understanding tasks by standardizing inputs/outputs into token sequences and using multi-scale spatiotemporal perception networks.
AI.motivation: Current audio-visual tasks (event localization, parsing, segmentation, question answering) are explored individually, making it hard to comprehensively understand complex scenes and explore inter-task relationships.
AI.method: 1) Standardize diverse task inputs/outputs into sequences of discrete tokens for shared representation; 2) Multi-scale temporal perception module for varying event granularity; 3) Cross-modal guidance-based spatial perception module for audio-visual associations; 4) Task-specific text prompts for adaptability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (AVE, LLP, MUSIC-AVQA, VGG-SS, AVS) demonstrate effectiveness across temporal, spatial, and spatiotemporal tasks.
AI.conclusion: AV-Unified enables comprehensive audio-visual scene understanding through unified joint learning, capturing complex inter-task relationships that individual approaches miss.

title: NEGATE: Constrained Semantic Guidance for Linguistic Negation in Text-to-Video Diffusion
summary: Negation is a fundamental linguistic operator, yet it remains inadequately modeled in diffusion-based generative systems. In this work, we present a formal treatment of linguistic negation in diffusion-based generative models by modeling it as a structured feasibility constraint on semantic guidance within diffusion dynamics. Rather than introducing heuristics or retraining model parameters, we reinterpret classifier-free guidance as defining a semantic update direction and enforce negation by projecting the update onto a convex constraint set derived from linguistic structure. This novel formulation provides a unified framework for handling diverse negation phenomena, including object absence, graded non-inversion semantics, multi-negation composition, and scope-sensitive disambiguation. Our approach is training-free, compatible with pretrained diffusion backbones, and naturally extends from image generation to temporally evolving video trajectories. In addition, we introduce a structured negation-centric benchmark suite that isolates distinct linguistic failure modes in generative systems, to further research in this area. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves robust negation compliance while preserving visual fidelity and structural coherence, establishing the first unified formulation of linguistic negation in diffusion-based generative models beyond representation-level evaluation.

AI.tldr: Formal treatment of linguistic negation in diffusion models as structured feasibility constraints on semantic guidance, enabling training-free negation handling across diverse phenomena.
AI.motivation: Negation is a fundamental linguistic operator but remains inadequately modeled in diffusion-based generative systems, creating a gap in handling diverse negation phenomena like object absence, graded semantics, and scope-sensitive disambiguation.
AI.method: Reinterpret classifier-free guidance as semantic update direction and enforce negation by projecting updates onto convex constraint sets derived from linguistic structure. Training-free approach compatible with pretrained diffusion backbones, extending to both images and videos.
AI.result: Method achieves robust negation compliance while preserving visual fidelity and structural coherence. Introduces structured negation-centric benchmark suite to isolate distinct linguistic failure modes in generative systems.
AI.conclusion: Establishes first unified formulation of linguistic negation in diffusion-based generative models beyond representation-level evaluation, providing formal framework for diverse negation phenomena without retraining.

title: SurgFormer: Scalable Learning of Organ Deformation with Resection Support and Real-Time Inference
summary: We introduce SurgFormer, a multiresolution gated transformer for data driven soft tissue simulation on volumetric meshes. High fidelity biomechanical solvers are often too costly for interactive use, so we train SurgFormer on solver generated data to predict nodewise displacement fields at near real time rates. SurgFormer builds a fixed mesh hierarchy and applies repeated multibranch blocks that combine local message passing, coarse global self attention, and pointwise feedforward updates, fused by learned per node, per channel gates to adaptively integrate local and long range information while remaining scalable on large meshes. For cut conditioned simulation, resection information is encoded as a learned cut embedding and provided as an additional input, enabling a unified model for both standard deformation prediction and topology altering cases. We also introduce two surgical simulation datasets generated under a unified protocol with XFEM based supervision: a cholecystectomy resection dataset and an appendectomy manipulation and resection dataset with cut and uncut cases. To our knowledge, this is the first learned volumetric surrogate setting to study XFEM supervised cut conditioned deformation within the same volumetric pipeline as standard deformation prediction. Across diverse baselines, SurgFormer achieves strong accuracy with favorable efficiency, making it a practical backbone for both tasks. {Code, data, and project page: \href{https://mint-vu.github.io/SurgFormer/}{available here}}

AI.tldr: SurgFormer is a multiresolution gated transformer that predicts soft tissue deformation on volumetric meshes at near real-time rates, handling both standard deformation and cut-conditioned simulation with learned cut embeddings.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity biomechanical solvers are too computationally expensive for interactive surgical simulation, creating a need for data-driven surrogates that can predict tissue deformation quickly while handling complex scenarios like surgical cuts.
AI.method: Uses a multiresolution gated transformer architecture with fixed mesh hierarchy, multibranch blocks combining local message passing, coarse global self-attention, and pointwise feedforward updates, fused by learned per-node gates. For cut-conditioned simulation, encodes resection information as learned cut embeddings.
AI.result: Achieves strong accuracy with favorable efficiency compared to diverse baselines, making it practical for both standard deformation prediction and topology-altering cases. Introduces two surgical simulation datasets (cholecystectomy and appendectomy) with XFEM-based supervision.
AI.conclusion: SurgFormer provides a scalable, efficient backbone for interactive surgical simulation that can handle both standard tissue deformation and complex cut-conditioned scenarios within a unified volumetric pipeline, representing the first learned approach to study XFEM-supervised cut-conditioned deformation.

title: Modeling and Measuring Redundancy in Multisource Multimodal Data for Autonomous Driving
comment: This paper has been accepted by the Fourth IEEE International Conference on Mobility: Operations, Services, and Technologies (MOST) 2026
summary: Next-generation autonomous vehicles (AVs) rely on large volumes of multisource and multimodal ($M^2$) data to support real-time decision-making. In practice, data quality (DQ) varies across sources and modalities due to environmental conditions and sensor limitations, yet AV research has largely prioritized algorithm design over DQ analysis. This work focuses on redundancy as a fundamental but underexplored DQ issue in AV datasets. Using the nuScenes and Argoverse 2 (AV2) datasets, we model and measure redundancy in multisource camera data and multimodal image-LiDAR data, and evaluate how removing redundant labels affects the YOLOv8 object detection task. Experimental results show that selectively removing redundant multisource image object labels from cameras with shared fields of view improves detection. In nuScenes, mAP${50}$ gains from $0.66$ to $0.70$, $0.64$ to $0.67$, and from $0.53$ to $0.55$, on three representative overlap regions, while detection on other overlapping camera pairs remains at the baseline even under stronger pruning. In AV2, $4.1$-$8.6\%$ of labels are removed, and mAP${50}$ stays near the $0.64$ baseline. Multimodal analysis also reveals substantial redundancy between image and LiDAR data. These findings demonstrate that redundancy is a measurable and actionable DQ factor with direct implications for AV performance. This work highlights the role of redundancy as a data quality factor in AV perception and motivates a data-centric perspective for evaluating and improving AV datasets. Code, data, and implementation details are publicly available at: https://github.com/yhZHOU515/RedundancyAD

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes redundancy as a data quality factor in autonomous vehicle datasets, showing that removing redundant labels from multisource camera data can improve object detection performance.
AI.motivation: Autonomous vehicles rely on multisource multimodal data, but data quality varies across sources and modalities. While AV research has focused on algorithm design, redundancy as a fundamental data quality issue remains underexplored.
AI.method: The study models and measures redundancy in multisource camera data and multimodal image-LiDAR data using nuScenes and Argoverse 2 datasets. It evaluates how removing redundant labels affects YOLOv8 object detection performance.
AI.result: Selectively removing redundant multisource image object labels improves detection: in nuScenes, mAP50 gains from 0.66 to 0.70, 0.64 to 0.67, and 0.53 to 0.55 on three overlap regions. In AV2, 4.1-8.6% of labels are removed while mAP50 stays near the 0.64 baseline. Multimodal analysis reveals substantial redundancy between image and LiDAR data.
AI.conclusion: Redundancy is a measurable and actionable data quality factor with direct implications for AV performance. The work highlights redundancy's role in AV perception and motivates a data-centric perspective for evaluating and improving AV datasets.

title: EgoReasoner: Learning Egocentric 4D Reasoning via Task-Adaptive Structured Thinking
summary: Egocentric video understanding is inherently complex due to the dynamic 4D nature of the environment, where camera motion and object displacements necessitate a continuous re-evaluation of spatial relations. In this work, we target a suite of under-explored egocentric 4D reasoning tasks, including fixture interaction counting, viewpoint-relative fixture location, object movement itinerary tracking, and stationary object localization, that require fundamentally different cognitive operations: spatial anchoring, temporal tracking, and duration reasoning. We observe that these structural differences make task-agnostic approaches insufficient: generic Chain-of-Thought methods lack task-appropriate reasoning primitives, and uniform reinforcement learning actively destabilizes performance on spatial tasks. To address this, we propose EgoReasoner, a two-stage framework that aligns both the reasoning scaffold and the reward signal to each task's cognitive structure. In the first stage, Task-Adaptive Thinking Templates guide the synthesis of structured CoT traces that teach the model to reason adaptively across task types via supervised fine-tuning. In the second stage, task-aware reward functions verify entity grounding, temporal alignment, and task-adaptive logical consistency, selectively strengthening each reasoning pathway via reinforcement fine-tuning with GRPO. Our 3B-parameter model, trained on only 16K samples, achieves 37.5% average accuracy on the challenging HD-EPIC benchmark, surpassing Qwen2.5-VL-7B (25.7%) by over 10 points.

AI.tldr: EgoReasoner: A two-stage framework for egocentric 4D reasoning that adapts reasoning scaffolds and rewards to different cognitive tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance on HD-EPIC benchmark.
AI.motivation: Egocentric video understanding is complex due to dynamic 4D environments requiring continuous spatial re-evaluation. Existing task-agnostic approaches (generic Chain-of-Thought, uniform reinforcement learning) are insufficient for diverse cognitive operations needed for tasks like fixture interaction counting, viewpoint-relative fixture location, object movement tracking, and stationary object localization.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: 1) Task-Adaptive Thinking Templates guide structured CoT trace synthesis for supervised fine-tuning, teaching adaptive reasoning across task types. 2) Task-aware reward functions verify entity grounding, temporal alignment, and logical consistency for reinforcement fine-tuning with GRPO, selectively strengthening each reasoning pathway.
AI.result: 3B-parameter model trained on only 16K samples achieves 37.5% average accuracy on HD-EPIC benchmark, surpassing Qwen2.5-VL-7B (25.7%) by over 10 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Aligning reasoning scaffolds and reward signals to each task's cognitive structure is crucial for effective egocentric 4D reasoning, enabling a single model to handle diverse cognitive operations with high efficiency and performance.

title: Penguin-VL: Exploring the Efficiency Limits of VLM with LLM-based Vision Encoders
summary: Vision Language Model (VLM) development has largely relied on scaling model size, which hinders deployment on compute-constrained mobile and edge devices such as smartphones and robots. In this work, we explore the performance limits of compact (e.g., 2B and 8B) VLMs. We challenge the prevailing practice that state-of-the-art VLMs must rely on vision encoders initialized via massive contrastive pretraining (e.g., CLIP/SigLIP). We identify an objective mismatch: contrastive learning, optimized for discrimination, enforces coarse and category-level invariances that suppress fine-grained visual cues needed for dense captioning and complex VLM reasoning. To address this issue, we present Penguin-VL, whose vision encoder is initialized from a text-only LLM. Our experiments reveal that Penguin-Encoder serves as a superior alternative to traditional contrastive pretraining, unlocking a higher degree of visual fidelity and data efficiency for multimodal understanding. Across various image and video benchmarks, Penguin-VL achieves performance comparable to leading VLMs (e.g., Qwen3-VL) in mathematical reasoning and surpasses them in tasks such as document understanding, visual knowledge, and multi-perspective video understanding. Notably, these gains are achieved with a lightweight architecture, demonstrating that improved visual representation rather than model scaling is the primary driver of performance. Our ablations show that Penguin-Encoder consistently outperforms contrastive-pretrained encoders, preserving fine-grained spatial and temporal cues that are critical for dense perception and complex reasoning. This makes it a strong drop-in alternative for compute-efficient VLMs and enables high performance in resource-constrained settings. Code: https://github.com/tencent-ailab/Penguin-VL

AI.tldr: Penguin-VL challenges the need for contrastive-pretrained vision encoders in VLMs, showing that text-only LLM initialization enables better visual fidelity and efficiency for compact models.
AI.motivation: Current VLM development relies on scaling model size, which limits deployment on mobile/edge devices. There's an objective mismatch where contrastive pretraining (CLIP/SigLIP) suppresses fine-grained visual cues needed for dense captioning and complex reasoning.
AI.method: Penguin-VL uses a vision encoder initialized from a text-only LLM instead of contrastive-pretrained encoders. This approach preserves fine-grained spatial and temporal cues critical for dense perception and complex reasoning.
AI.result: Penguin-VL achieves performance comparable to leading VLMs (e.g., Qwen3-VL) in mathematical reasoning and surpasses them in document understanding, visual knowledge, and multi-perspective video understanding, all with lightweight architecture.
AI.conclusion: Improved visual representation rather than model scaling is the primary driver of performance. Penguin-Encoder serves as a superior drop-in alternative to contrastive-pretrained encoders for compute-efficient VLMs in resource-constrained settings.

title: SCOPE: Scene-Contextualized Incremental Few-Shot 3D Segmentation
summary: Incremental Few-Shot (IFS) segmentation aims to learn new categories over time from only a few annotations. Although widely studied in 2D, it remains underexplored for 3D point clouds. Existing methods suffer from catastrophic forgetting or fail to learn discriminative prototypes under sparse supervision, and often overlook a key cue: novel categories frequently appear as unlabelled background in base-training scenes. We introduce SCOPE (Scene-COntextualised Prototype Enrichment), a plug-and-play background-guided prototype enrichment framework that integrates with any prototype-based 3D segmentation method. After base training, a class-agnostic segmentation model extracts high-confidence pseudo-instances from background regions to build a prototype pool. When novel classes arrive with few labelled samples, relevant background prototypes are retrieved and fused with few-shot prototypes to form enriched representations without retraining the backbone or adding parameters. Experiments on ScanNet and S3DIS show that SCOPE achieves SOTA performance, improving novel-class IoU by up to 6.98% and 3.61%, and mean IoU by 2.25% and 1.70%, respectively, while maintaining low forgetting. Code is available https://github.com/Surrey-UP-Lab/SCOPE.

AI.tldr: SCOPE is a plug-and-play framework for 3D incremental few-shot segmentation that enriches novel class prototypes using background pseudo-instances from base training scenes, achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal forgetting.
AI.motivation: 3D incremental few-shot segmentation is underexplored compared to 2D, and existing methods suffer from catastrophic forgetting, poor discriminative prototypes under sparse supervision, and overlook that novel categories often appear as unlabelled background in base-training scenes.
AI.method: SCOPE uses a class-agnostic segmentation model after base training to extract high-confidence pseudo-instances from background regions to build a prototype pool. When novel classes arrive with few samples, relevant background prototypes are retrieved and fused with few-shot prototypes to create enriched representations without retraining the backbone or adding parameters.
AI.result: On ScanNet and S3DIS datasets, SCOPE achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving novel-class IoU by up to 6.98% and 3.61%, and mean IoU by 2.25% and 1.70% respectively, while maintaining low forgetting.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE effectively leverages background information from base training scenes to enrich few-shot prototypes for novel classes, providing a plug-and-play solution that significantly improves 3D incremental few-shot segmentation performance without architectural changes or retraining.

title: Omni-Diffusion: Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation with Masked Discrete Diffusion
summary: While recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made impressive strides, they predominantly employ a conventional autoregressive architecture as their backbone, leaving significant room to explore effective and efficient alternatives in architectural design. Concurrently, recent studies have successfully applied discrete diffusion models to various domains, such as visual understanding and image generation, revealing their considerable potential as a promising backbone for multimodal systems. Drawing inspiration from these pioneering research, we introduce Omni-Diffusion, the first any-to-any multimodal language model built entirely on mask-based discrete diffusion models, which unifies understanding and generation across text, speech, and images. Omni-Diffusion employs a unified mask-based discrete diffusion model to directly capture the joint distribution over discrete multimodal tokens. This approach supports not only bimodal tasks but also more complex scenarios involving multiple modalities. On a diverse set of benchmarks, our method outperforms or performs on par with existing multimodal systems that process two or more modalities, highlighting the significant promise of diffusion models in powering the next generation of multimodal foundation models. Project webpage: https://omni-diffusion.github.io.

AI.tldr: Omni-Diffusion is the first any-to-any multimodal language model built entirely on mask-based discrete diffusion models, unifying understanding and generation across text, speech, and images.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) predominantly use conventional autoregressive architectures, leaving room to explore more effective and efficient alternatives. Discrete diffusion models have shown promise in visual understanding and image generation, suggesting their potential as a backbone for multimodal systems.
AI.method: Omni-Diffusion employs a unified mask-based discrete diffusion model to directly capture the joint distribution over discrete multimodal tokens. This approach supports bimodal tasks and more complex scenarios involving multiple modalities.
AI.result: On diverse benchmarks, Omni-Diffusion outperforms or performs on par with existing multimodal systems that process two or more modalities.
AI.conclusion: The work highlights the significant promise of diffusion models in powering the next generation of multimodal foundation models, demonstrating that discrete diffusion models can serve as an effective alternative to conventional autoregressive architectures for multimodal systems.

title: Multimodal Large Language Models as Image Classifiers
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLM) classification performance depends critically on evaluation protocol and ground truth quality. Studies comparing MLLMs with supervised and vision-language models report conflicting conclusions, and we show these conflicts stem from protocols that either inflate or underestimate performance. Across the most common evaluation protocols, we identify and fix key issues: model outputs that fall outside the provided class list and are discarded, inflated results from weak multiple-choice distractors, and an open-world setting that underperforms only due to poor output mapping. We additionally quantify the impact of commonly overlooked design choices - batch size, image ordering, and text encoder selection - showing they substantially affect accuracy. Evaluating on ReGT, our multilabel reannotation of 625 ImageNet-1k classes, reveals that MLLMs benefit most from corrected labels (up to +10.8%), substantially narrowing the perceived gap with supervised models. Much of the reported MLLMs underperformance on classification is thus an artifact of noisy ground truth and flawed evaluation protocol rather than genuine model deficiency. Models less reliant on supervised training signals prove most sensitive to annotation quality. Finally, we show that MLLMs can assist human annotators: in a controlled case study, annotators confirmed or integrated MLLMs predictions in approximately 50% of difficult cases, demonstrating their potential for large-scale dataset curation.

AI.tldr: MLLM classification performance gaps are largely due to flawed evaluation protocols and noisy ground truth, not genuine model deficiencies. Fixing these issues narrows the gap with supervised models, and MLLMs can assist in dataset curation.
AI.motivation: Conflicting conclusions about MLLM classification performance compared to supervised models stem from problematic evaluation protocols and poor ground truth quality. The paper aims to identify and fix these issues to provide accurate performance assessment.
AI.method: Identifies and fixes key issues in common evaluation protocols: handling model outputs outside class lists, addressing weak multiple-choice distractors, and improving output mapping. Quantifies impact of overlooked design choices (batch size, image ordering, text encoder). Evaluates on ReGT, a multilabel reannotation of 625 ImageNet-1k classes. Conducts controlled case study on MLLM-assisted human annotation.
AI.result: MLLMs benefit most from corrected labels (up to +10.8% improvement), substantially narrowing the perceived gap with supervised models. Much reported underperformance is artifact of noisy ground truth and flawed protocols. Models less reliant on supervised training are most sensitive to annotation quality. MLLMs can assist human annotators, with approximately 50% of difficult cases confirmed or integrated.
AI.conclusion: MLLM classification underperformance is largely due to evaluation artifacts rather than genuine model deficiencies. Proper evaluation protocols and high-quality ground truth reveal MLLMs perform much better than previously reported. MLLMs show promise for large-scale dataset curation through human-in-the-loop annotation assistance.
